name: 'Ain el-Sabil Church
description: Late antique mud-brick church located at Ain el-Sabil in Egypt. The exact dating of the site is still unknown.

name: 'Awām
description: The ruins of the Sabaean temple dedicated to Almaqah and its associated burial ground are located near the town of Mārib, Yemen.

name: 'Ein Gedi
description: A Ghassulian public building dating ca. 3500 B.C. near the oasis of Ein Gedi on the western shore of the Dead Sea.

name: 'Evron
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 B4 'Evron

name: ‘Herculis Rani’?
description: A large fanum sacred to ‘Herculis Rani’? at Campochiaro, the name of which is then preserved in the station mentioned on the Peutinger Table. Scholars like Adriano La Regina support linking this fanum to a place mentioned by Livy, although see the discussion by Oakley.

name: ‘Iovis Lareni’
description: ‘Iovis Lareni’ is marked as a station on the Peutinger map. It lies 7 miles from Sulmo. This place coincides with the ancient site of Ocriticum, a pre-Roman and Roman settlement. The site, located in the Italian comune of Cansano (province of L'Aquila, Abruzzo), includes the remains of two Italo-Roman temples sacred to Jupiter and Hercules and a sacellum of Ceres and Venus.

name: ‘Lavobrinta’?
description: ‘Lavobrinta’? was a Roman fort on the east bank of the Severn river.

name: ‘Trebula’
description: A pre-Roman site in the valley of the Sangro river that was later converted to a Christian church.

name: "Basilica" of San Vitale
description: A church (but not a basilica) in Ravenna, Italy, established by Bishop Ecclesius in 526 AD and consecrated ca. 547 AD.

name: [Augusta] Ambianorum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B2 [Augusta] Ambianorum

name: [Camelaria]
description: Numerous ex-votos were excavated from a spring at La Source des Roches.

name: [Hagios Prokopios]
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 63 F3 [Hagios Prokopios]

name: [Per-ousekh]
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D3 [Per-ousekh]

name: [Wadi Sarga]
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E3 [Wadi Sarga]

name: *Brocolitia
description: Roman fort near present-day Carrawburgh.

name: *Calanta
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B3 *Calanta

name: *Chrononum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I3 *Chrononum

name: *Ganuenta?
description: Colijnsplaat, The Netherlands, is the site of an ancient sanctuary dedicated to Nehalennia.

name: *Hercules Curinus
description: A sanctuary in the territory of the Paeligni dedicated to Hercules Curinus situated on an artificial terrace on the slopes of Mount Morrone. The terrace sanctuary looks over Sulmo in the valley below.

name: *Iciacum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I3 *Iciacum

name: *Incerulae
description: *Incerulae, an ancient sanctuary site in the territory of the Vestini.

name: *Karistoron
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 49 E1 *Karistoron

name: *Manatum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H2 *Manatum

name: *Marciacum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I3 *Marciacum

name: *Maroialum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G1 *Maroialum

name: *Melita
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H3 *Melita

name: *Novivicum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 F1 *Novivicum

name: *Panamara
description: *Panamara was as site in Caria that was a dependency of Stratonikeia and site of the sanctuary of Zeus Panamaros.

name: *Pauliacum, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G2 *Pauliacum, Mon.

name: *Porsulae/Maximianopolis
description: *Porsulae/Maximianopolis, also known as Mosynopolis, was a Byzantine town along the Via Egnatia in Thrace; its position is close to the modern Greek city of Komotini.

name: *Qubba
description: A monastery constructed in the fifth century CE on the site of a cave previously inhabited by St. Paul the Anchorite.

name: *Randanum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I3 *Randanum

name: *Sioua
description: "Sioua" is the Greek reconstruction of an ancient toponym (found in Egyptian sources: S·t-wȝḥ) that encompassed some part of the western end of the Dakhla Oasis in Egypt (certainly the site now known as Deir al-Haggar was included, and possibly also Amheida).

name: *Siroialum?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 F3 *Siroialum?

name: *Templum Augusti
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 5 D4 *Templum Augusti

name: *Vanisnesi
description: Extensive Roman ruins identified by Gsell (AAA, whence BAtlas) at a site he called Oum Sekak in the Hassenaoua district of modern Algeria. He estimates the site extent at 13 hectares and reports the remains of a church. An inscription that seems to provide the name of the "genius loci" (Genius Vanisnesi) was found in the vicinity. The site is not clearly visible in twenty-first century aerial/satellite imagery and most of the modern toponymy Gsell provides does not appear in the open-source, digital gazetteers and map tools. The site may now lie under modern development in the area.

name: Abaton
description: An ancient site designated as the burial place of Osiris-Serapis.

name: Abba Garima Monastery
description: The Abba Garima Monastery is an Ethiopian Orthodox church founded in the sixth century CE by Abba Garima, one of the so-called Nine Saints. It is located east of Adwa, in the Mehakelegnaw Zone of the northern Tigray Region of Ethiopia.

name: Abbaye Saint-Pierre de Moissac
description: An important monastery founded probably in the seventh century of the common era.

name: Abbazia di San Giovanni in Venere
description: A Christian monastic complex that is located in the municipality of Fossacesia, Italy. The site occupies a hilltop at 107 m above sea level on the Adriatic Sea coast. The basilica dates originally from the thirteenth century.

name: Abbazia di San Vincenzo al Volturno
description: An historic Benedictine monastery located in the Province of Isernia, near the source of the river Volturno.

name: Abbazia San Claudio al Chienti
description: The church of San Claudio al Chienti is a Romanesque church building located in the municipality of Corridonia in the Chienti valley. It was founded in the eleventh century.

name: Abbey of Monte Cassino
description: A multi-period hilltop settlement with evidence for ancient terrace construction that now supports an abbey of the Benedictine order.

name: Abbeyknockmoy abbey
description: A ruined Medieval Cistercian abbey founded in 1190.

name: Abini nuragic sanctuary
description: A nuragic sanctuary.

name: Abu Simbel
description: The site of two temples constructed by Ramesses II, one dedicated to the triad of Amun, Ra-Horakhty, and Ptah, as well as the deified Ramesses, and another dedicated to Hathor and the pharaoh's wife Nefertari. The temple was relocated between 1964 and 1968 to prevent its submersion under Lake Nasser after the construction of the Aswan High Dam.

name: Abydos/Ebot
description: An ancient Egyptian city and the burial place of its first kings. Primary cult place of the god Osiris.

name: Achna
description: Achna is a modern village on Cyprus where evidence exists for an ancient sanctuary dedicated to a goddess perhaps associated with Artemis.

name: Acropolis of Cumae
description: The Acropolis of Cumae was used across multiple periods, beginning with the foundation of the Greek colony in the eighth century B.C.

name: Actium
description: Site of an ancient temple of Apollo near which Octavian erected a monument to commemorate his naval victory over M. Antonius in 31 BCE.

name: Ad Herculem
description: An ancient station on the Roman road to Gades recorded in the Antonine Itinerary, ad Herculem must have been associated with the Herakleion on the southern end of the island of Cotinussa. Scholars differ on the precise location; their efforts are complicated by significant landscape change since antiquity.

name: Adad temple (Khorsabad)
description: As part of the palatial complex of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) constructed six temples, including one to the god Adad.

name: Adad-Šala temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the god Adad and the goddess Šala. Although its location is not known, the Adad-Šala temple at Kalhu is thought to have been situated within the Ninurta temple complex, in the northwest corner of the citadel.

name: Aedes Apollinis Palatini
description: The temple sacred to the god Apollo on the Palatine Hill in Rome.

name: Aedes Ceres Liber Liberaque
description: The Temple of Ceres, located on the slope of the Aventine Hill in Rome, was originally dedicated by Sp. Cassius in 494/3 B.C.

name: Aedes Fauni
description: A temple dedicated to Faunus located on Tiber Island, the Aedes Fauni was dedicated in 196 BCE by Gnaeus Domitius Ahenobarbus and Gaius Scribonius Libo.

name: Aedes Herculis Pompeiani
description: A temple of Hercules located near the Circus Maximus.

name: Aedes Salus
description: While consul in 311 BCE, Caius Iunius Bubulcus Brutus vowed the temple of Salus; he would later dedicate it as dictator in 302 BCE. While no physical remains of the temple are known, textual and epigraphic testimony suggest that it would have been located near the present-day Piazza del Quirinale.

name: Aedes Serapis
description: Caracalla dedicated a temple to Serapis in Regio VI. Its dedication is confirmed via epigraphic elements and some of its architectural elements are also known. It was located on the Collis Latiaris, which is the southern most part of the Quirinal Hill.

name: Aedes Tensarum
description: A temple on Rome's Capitoline Hill where the carts that carried the exuviae in festival parades were stored.

name: Aefula
description: Aefula was an ancient settlement of Latium that had disappeared by the time of Pliny the Elder, although the cult of Bona Dea there continued to flourish.

name: Ag. Andreas (Siphnos)
description: A Classical-Hellenistic period settlement on/around the site of an earlier Mycenaean citadel on the island of Siphnos, now partially occupied by the church of Agios Andreas.

name: Ag. Demetrios
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 C3 Ag. Demetrios

name: Ag. Dimitrios
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 inset Ag. Dimitrios

name: Ag. Eirene
description: Ayia Irini, located on Keos, was occupied from the Late Neolithic until the Mycenaean period. The site was extensively excavated under J.L. Caskey.

name: Ag. Eirini
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 B2 Ag. Eirini

name: Ag. Ioannis Rodakis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Ag. Ioannis Rodakis

name: Ag. Isidoros
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C3 Ag. Isidoros

name: Ag. Lot
description: The Sanctuary of Agios Lot is located at the southeastern end of the Dead Sea.

name: Ag. Mavri
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 B3 Ag. Mavri

name: Ag. Nikitas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 C4 Ag. Nikitas

name: Ag. Panagia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 D2 Ag. Panagia

name: Ag. Sophia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Ag. Sophia

name: Ag. Stephanos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 B3 Ag. Stephanos

name: Ag. Thomas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Ag. Thomas

name: Ag. Trias
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 E1 Ag. Trias

name: Ag. Tykhonas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 C3 Ag. Tykhonas

name: Agamemnoneion of Mycenae
description: An archaeological site near the Mycenaean causeway has been interpreted as a multi-period heroön dedicated to Agamemnon.

name: Agdistis Sanctuary (Yazılıkaya)
description: A plateau around a monumental Phrygian rock‐cut altar at the extensive fortified settlement and sanctuary complex of Yazılıkaya in Turkey. Associated with Agdistis in the 20th century because of Roman epigraphic finds there, more recent excavations have demonstrated that the sanctuary was in use from the 8th–7th century BCE to the Roman Period (1st-2nd century CE). Continuity of ritual practice in front of the rock-cut altar is demonstrated by rock-cut bows, a ritual preparation area with hearths, a rock-cut statue identifiable as the goddess Matar, and the Roman-era votive inscriptions.

name: Agia Dynamis
description: A sixteenth-century chapel in Athens built atop an ancient shrine of Herakles.

name: Agios Elias Doric temple
description: The Doric temple at Agios Elias, above Asea in Arcadia, dates to ca. 500 B.C.

name: Agnone
description: Agnone is a comune in the Italian province of Isernia, in the Molise region. It lies about 53 km (33 miles) northwest of Campobasso. An ancient center of Oscan-Samnite culture, Agnone is known particularly as the namesake of the third-century BCE "Agnone tablet" that was discovered at Fonte di Romito.

name: Agora of Halaesa
description: The agora of the ancient city of Halaesa (second century B.C.), now within the archaeological area of Alesa Arconidea, Sicily.

name: Agueneb
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 29 G3 Agueneb

name: Ai Lia temples
description: An ancient Greek sanctuary located at ancient Keryneia in Achaia.

name: Aiakeion
description: The Aiakeion was a sanctuary of Aiakos built in Athens in the early fifth century BC, which is identified with the Rectangular Peribolos in the southwestern corner of the Agora. In 374/3 BC it was converted to a granary. It was damaged in the Sullan Sack in 86 BC and in the Imperial period it was a pottery workshop.

name: Aigaion Antron
description: A cave on Crete sacred to the Minoans that may be the Diktaean cave, the putative site of Zeus' birth in Greek mythology.

name: Ain Aata
description: Sanctuary of the Roman period

name: Ain Dara temple
description: The Ain Dara temple, an archaeological site located near the village of Ain Dara, in Afrin, Syria, was the location of an Iron Age Syro-Hittite temple. The structure was noted for the similarities it bore to Solomon's Temple in Jerusalem. The site was in use from ca. 1300 until 740 BCE. It finds comparison with the temples at Emar, Mumbaqa, and Ebla's Temple D. In January 2018 the ruins of the temple were substantially damaged by air strikes carried out by the Turkish Air Force during the Afrin offensive. The site also subsequently suffered further damage, wanton destruction, and looting.

name: Ain el Aouad
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 Ain el Aouad

name: Ain Hersha Roman Temple
description: Ain Hersha is the site of a Roman temple located in the environs of Mount Hermon.

name: Ain Nagila
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D4 Ain Nagila

name: Ain Zeitun
description: An ancient place, cited: None

name: Aix-en-Diois
description: Among various features at Aix-en-Diois are the remains of baths that could either pertain to a sanctuary or a villa.

name: Ajanta Caves
description: The Ajanta Caves are among the earliest Buddhist cave monuments and date from the second and first centuries B.C. During the Gupta period (fifth and sixth centuries A.D.), additional decorated caves were added to the group.

name: Akītu of Šarrat-Nippuri
description: Temple in Babylon, not located, but probably not far from Eḫursagtila, for it is mentioned together in the ritual texts of the "Divine Love Lyrics". Šarrat-Nippuri "The Queen of Nippur" was a hypostasis of Ištar. It is difficult to ascertain the precise reason for the existence of an Akītu temple dedicated to this deity in the city of Babylon.

name: Akītu-Temple of Ištar
description: The New Year’s House (or Akītu Temple) of Nineveh's tutelary deity Ištar appears to date back at least to the Middle Assyrian period (reign of Tukultī-Ninurta I). Its Sumerian ceremonial name is not preserved in extant cuneiform sources. This temple's exact location remains unknown and it is certain whether Ištar's Akītu Temple was located in the citadel or in the lower town.

name: Akoimeton Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 B2 Akoimeton Mon.

name: Akragas/Agrigentum
description: Akragas/Agrigentum was an ancient city of Sicily and is now known as Agrigento. The Archaeological Area of Agrigento was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1997.

name: Akrokorinthos
description: Akrokorinthos (Acrocorinth) was the acropolis of ancient Corinth.

name: Aksu - Zindan Mağarası Sanctuary
description: An ancient sanctuary complex, incorporating a cave known today as Zindan Mağarası, which overlooks a bend in the river Aksu (locally Zindan Deresi; a tributary of the Eurymedon) in Turkey's Isparta province. Archaeological and epigraphic evidence demonstrate human activity from the Hellenistic to the Seljuk period, with the dedication of a temple to the Vigenon Mother of the Gods during the reign of Marcus Aurelius. A Roman-era bridge and road section has also been identified as part of the complex, which was probably associated with the nearby city of Timbriada. The sanctuary also honored the god Eurymedon. A Christian monastery succeeded the sanctuary, spoliating its buildings for construction material.

name: al-Husn Church
description: A ruined and now largely dispersed fifth-century CE church on the outskirts of al-Bara, Syria.

name: Alahan
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 66 C3 Alahan

name: Alahana Parivena
description: A middle to late eleventh century CE monastic college located outside of Polonnaruwa in Sri Lanka that belonged to King Parakramabahu I.

name: Albanella
description: A rural sanctuary in the territory of Poseidonia (Paestum) has been excavated at S. Nicola di Albanella.

name: Alcántara
description: The Roman temple of Alcántara is a well-preserved structure located at one side of the Alcántara Bridge in Extremadura (Spain). The building, dating to A.D. 103, is the work of Caius Iulius Lacer. The lintel inscription indicates the structure was dedicated to Trajan.

name: Aliphera sanctuary
description: Pausanias observed sanctuaries of both Asclepius and Athena at Aliphera, as well as an altar of Zeus Lecheates.

name: Allan
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D5 Allan

name: Allègre
description: The site of sulphur springs that also revealed evidence for a sanctuary that included 24 altars, so of which carried dedications either to nymphs or mother goddesses. A number of Roman artifacts indicate activity at the site from the Flavian to the Antonine period.

name: Allonzier-la-Caille
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 G2 Allonzier-la-Caille

name: Almendres Cromlech
description: A megalithic complex located near Guadalupe, Portgual, dating to the Early Neolithic period.

name: Alsos Asklepiou
description: A seventh- and eighth-century BC Punic sanctuary on the Awali river dedicated to Eshmun, the Phoenician god of healing.

name: Altar at the temple of Amphiaraos
description: An altar located to the east of the temple of Amphiaraos.

name: Altar of Hieron II
description: A monumental altar built around the third century B.C. that was probably dedicated to Zeus Eleutherios. Remains of the altar are located in the archaeological area of Neapolis in Syracuse, Sicily.

name: Altar of the Twelve Gods
description: A rectangular peribolos with an altar in the centre, built in 522/1 BC, destroyed in the Persian Sack of 480/79 BC, and rebuilt in the late 5th century BC. It was the central milestone from which distances in Attica were measured. The remains are now mostly underneath the Athens-Piraeus Electric Railway.

name: Altar of Zeus Agoraios
description: An altar dedicated to Zeus Agoraios, originally erected on the Pnyx in the late fourth century BC, but relocated to the Athenian Agora in the Augustan period.

name: Altar of Zeus Polieus
description: An altar and sanctuary on the Acropolis of Athens, site of the bouphonia ritual during the Dipolieia festival. The shrine is to be identified with a set of cuttings at the highest point on the Acropolis, northeast of the Parthenon.

name: Alto da Vigia
description: A Roman sanctuary located at the mouth of the Colares river.

name: Altuncebbar
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 E5 Altuncebbar

name: Amaras
description: An ancient settlement and early Christian site, now occupied partly by an Armenian Apostolic monastery and its 18th century church.

name: Amarna
description: A new capital for Egypt founded by Akhenaten during his reign as pharaoh during Egypt's Eighteenth Dynasty.

name: Ameria (Pontos)
description: An ancient settlement, associated with the sanctuary of Men Pharnakou in Pontos. It was located near modern near Ardıçlı (formerly Agustu) in Turkey's Tokat province.

name: Ammon
description: The Siwa Oasis was, in antiquity, the site of the oracular temple of Amun (Zeus Ammon).

name: Ampass early Christian Church
description: The high altar of the present, Gothic-era Pfarrkirche (parish church) of modern Ampass (near Insbruck in Austria) was built over a series of earlier Christian churches, the earliest of which appears to date to the fifth century CE, based on excavated remains of a reliquary vault dating back to that period.

name: Amphiareion
description: The Amphiareion of Oropos was an ancient sanctuary dedicated in the fifth century BCE to the hero Amphiaraos and located in the hills southeast of the port of Oropos.

name: Amphitheater of Durrës
description: The Roman amphitheater of Durrës dates to the reign of Trajan. A small chapel built inside the amphitheater has mosaics likely dating to the 6th-8th centuries CE.

name: Amphitheater of the Three Gauls
description: The Sanctuary of the Three Gauls (Tres Galliae) was a late first century B.C. sanctuary in Lugdunum; the amphitheater dates to 19 B.C.

name: Ampouria Parnassou
description: A cave sanctuary, dedicated to Pan and the Nymphs, located near modern Ag. Marina in Phocis and therefore also near the site of Psilo Kastro (possibly ancient Patronis).

name: Amyun
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 A5 Amyun

name: Anaktoron at Pantalika
description: The Anaktoron is an enigmatic megalithic building associated with the prehistoric Sicilian settlement of Pantalica; its polygonal blocks may date to the 12-11th c. B.C., but the current form of the building seems to be the result of Byzantine reconstruction.

name: Ancient sanctuary at Marion
description: An Iron Age sanctuary located at Peristeries in Polis Chrysochous, Cyprus, dedicated to the female goddess of Cyprus that was in use for half a millennium, from ca. 950 to ca. 450 BCE.

name: Andrianton
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 75 D2 Andrianton

name: Anemospilia
description: A Minoan temple (?) located on northern end of Mount Iuktas.

name: Angdisseon, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 E4 Angdisseon, T.

name: Angitiae Lucus
description: Angitiae Lucus refers to the grove of Angitia, a divine figure of the Marsi tribe.

name: Angkor Wat
description: The principal temple of Angkor, the capital city of the Khmer empire, Angkor Wat was constructed in the 12th century CE. Angkor was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage site in 1992.

name: Anthela
description: The Amphictyonic council met at the Shrine of Demeter at Anthela.

name: Antikythera Apollo Sanctuary
description: The probable sanctuary of Apollo Aegileos on the modern Aegean island of Antikythera is attested by the presence of a squared marble platform and various artifacts, including sculptural remains.

name: Antinoeion at Hadrian's Villa
description: A sacred precinct at the western end of Villa Hadriani, comprising two temples at opposite (north and south) ends of a plaza or garden, and (on the western edge of this complex) a small colonnaded hemicycle. Here, Hadrian may have planned to build a large, semi-circular temple-tomb for Antinous, in place of which his successors built the colonnaded hemicycle.

name: Antro delle Sorti
description: The "Cave of the Fates" at Praeneste is a sanctuary at the base of the city's terraced sanctuary of Fortuna. It was said to contain wooden oracular tablets.

name: Antron Kybeles/Mithra
description: A sanctuary of the goddess Meter and her companion Attis, located at modern Kapıkaya in Turkey, 10km northwest of Pergamon. The earliest finds date to the third century BCE. Archaeological finds indicate that Mithras became the principal deity worshipped here in Roman times, down to at least the 4th century CE.

name: Apa Abraham, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 G4 Apa Abraham, Mon.

name: Apa Apollo
description: A Coptic monastery founded ca. 385/390 located 80 km north of Asyut, near the village of Dashlout, Egypt.

name: Apa Apollo, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E2 Apa Apollo, Mon.

name: Apa Apollo, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E3 Apa Apollo, Mon.

name: Apa Bane, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 D1 Apa Bane, Mon.

name: Apa Djoore, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F3 Apa Djoore, Mon.

name: Apa Epiphanius, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 B2 Apa Epiphanius, Mon.

name: Apa Ioannes, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E2 Apa Ioannes, Mon.

name: Apa Makrobios, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E3 Apa Makrobios, Mon.

name: Apa Palemon, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 G4 Apa Palemon, Mon.

name: Apa Phoibammon (monastery)
description: The seventh-century monastery of St. Phoibammon was built on the upper terrace of the temple of Hatshepsut at Deir el-Bahri-

name: Apa Psoi, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F3 Apa Psoi, Mon.

name: Apa Psote, Mon./Amba Bisada
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F4 Apa Psote, Mon./Amba Bisada

name: Apa Senouthou (monastery)
description: The "White Monastery", located near modern Tahta and Sohag in Egypt.

name: Apa Souroutos, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E3 Apa Souroutos, Mon.

name: Aphaka (Temple of Aphrodite Aphakitis)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 A5 Aphrodite Aphakitis, T.

name: Aphrodision
description: Located outside the city walls of Pergamon, this temple, according to an inscription (OGIS 273-9), was supposedly the site of a victory of Attalos I over the Tolistogi, Galatians, and Antiochus Hierax. It was likely later destroyed by Philip V.

name: Aphrodision at Akrai
description: Temple dedicated to Aphrodite constructed in the 6th century BCE at the Greek colony of Akrai.

name: Aphrodite Akraia, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 F1 Aphrodite Akraia, T.

name: Aphrodite Golgia
description: An important sanctuary of Aphrodite in the ancient city of Golgoi on the island of Cyprus.

name: Aphrodite Paphia, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 C2 Aphrodite Paphia, T.

name: Aphrodite Phystiou, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 B3 Aphrodite Phystiou, T.

name: Aphrodite sanctuary at Dafni
description: An ancient sanctuary of the goddess Aphrodite, described by Pausanias and located at modern Dafni (Daphne) along the ancient sacred way from Athens to Eleusis.

name: Aphrodite sanctuary at Lokroi Epizephyrioi
description: The worship of Aphrodite is significant in the colonial city of Lokroi Epizephyrioi.

name: Aphrodite, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 C3 Aphrodite, T.

name: Aphrodites Lophos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 E3 Aphrodites Lophos

name: Apollo Agyates, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 C2 Apollo Agyates, T.

name: Apollo Alaeus (temple)
description: A sanctuary of Apollo near Punta Alice. Tradition ascribed its foundation to Philoctetes and it is said he dedicated his bow to Apollo.

name: Apollo Alasiotas, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 C2 Apollo Alasiotas, T.

name: Apollo Asgelatos (temple)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 B5 Apollo Asgelatos, T.

name: Apollo Axyros, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 H4 Apollo Axyros, T.

name: Apollo Cynneius
description: Sanctuary of Apollo Cynneius near or in Temnos, destroyed by Prusias II in 155 BCE. Its precise location is unknown today but certainly lies within Barrington Atlas Map 56 grid E4.

name: Apollo Erethimios, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 G3 Apollo Erethimios, T.

name: Apollo et Nymphae Nitrodes (temple)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E4 Apollo et Nymphae Nitrodes, T.

name: Apollo Hylates, T.
description: The temple of Apollo Hylates, located northwest of Kourion, was one of the most important cult centers of ancient Cyprus. The site was active from the eighth century BCE and until the fourth century CE.

name: Apollo Karios, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 F5 Apollo Karios, T.

name: Apollo Korythos Sanctuary
description: A multi-phase sanctuary of Apollo that begins in the archaic period. Pausanias visited the sanctuary and observed two cult statues of the god. One of these, sacred to Apollo Corynthus, was of wood. The other, sacred to Apollo Argeotas, was of bronze.

name: Apollo Lairmenos, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 B5 Apollo Lairmenos, T.

name: Apollo Leukatas, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Apollo Leukatas, T.

name: Apollo Mageirios, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 D2 Apollo Mageirios, T.

name: Apollo Maleatas, T.
description: A cult of Apollo Maleatas has been identified at Profitis Ilias, Kosmas thanks to epigraphic evidence.

name: Apollo Melanthios, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 B3 Apollo Melanthios, T.

name: Apollo Nasiotas, T.
description: A sanctuary dedicated to Apollo Nasiotas located on the islet of Apsiphia.

name: Apollo Tegyraios, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 E3 Apollo Tegyraios, T.

name: Apollo Thourios, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D4 Apollo Thourios, T.

name: Apollo-Grannus-Tempel
description: A Roman temple in Faimingen near Lauingen in Bavaria.

name: Apollo, Aedes
description: The likely site of the original sanctuary to Apollo at Rome. The visible remains belong to the Augustan phase.

name: Apollo, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 65 E3 Apollo, T.

name: Apollo, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 C2 Apollo, T.

name: Apollo, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 D3 Apollo, T.

name: Apollo, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 A1 Apollo, T.

name: Apollo, T.
description: Two early Christian basilicae were superimposed over the ancient sanctuary of Apollo Dalios.

name: Apollo, T.
description: The oracular shrine of Apollo Koropaios.

name: Apollo, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 E4 Apollo, T.

name: Apollo, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 F3 Apollo, T.

name: Apollo, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 G3 Apollo, T.

name: Apollo, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 C4 Apollo, T.

name: Apollo, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 E4 Apollo, T.

name: Apollo, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 D1 Apollo, T.

name: Apostoli, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 D2 Apostoli, Mon.

name: Aqaba
description: Aqaba - now Aaqbe - is a place located in the Beqaa Governorate in Lebanon. It is the site of a Roman temple that has been classified as belonging to the "Mount Hermon" temple group.

name: Aqaba Church
description: A pre-Constantinian Christian church located at ancient Aila in Jordan.

name: Aquae Segetae
description: An ancient settlement at modern Sceaux-du-Gâtinais in France (BAtlas 11 C4 Sceaux-du-Gâtinais)

name: Ara della Regina
description: A fourth to third century B.C. sanctuary at Tarquinia.

name: Ara Maxima Herculis
description: An early cult center connected with Herakles located in Rome's Forum Boarium.

name: Ara Pacis Augustae
description: The "Altar of Augustan Peace" was located in the Campus Martius of Rome. It was originally situated to the west of the Via Lata, near the eastern end of Augustus' Horologium. The reconstructed fragments of the altar are now housed in a museum located on the Tiber's banks in the Piazza Augusto Imperatore, a square created during the Fascist era in order to spotlight the ruins of the Mausoleum of Augustus.

name: Arachnaion (mountain)
description: A mountain of the Argolid with a higher western peak (Profitis Ilias, 1199 masl) and a lower eastern peak (Mavrovouni or Arna, 1139 masl). Material from the Mycenaean, Archaic, and Classical periods has been found on the western peak and identified with altars of Zeus and Hera described by Pausanias. Aeschylus mentions its use as a signal station.

name: Archaic Temple at Ai Lia
description: Badly damaged foundations of the peristyle of a large Doric temple of the 6th or early 5th c. BCE, the center of which is now occupied by the modern chapel of Profitis Ilias on a rise NE of ancient Kyreneia. This temple was probably destroyed during the earthquake of 373 BCE and replaced by a smaller temple to the west, built from its rubble. A monumental great altar has also been excavated immediately east of the temple.

name: Archbishop's Chapel, Ravenna
description: A private, early Christian oratory in Ravenna, Italy, erected by Peter II as archbishop in A.D. 495.

name: Archegetes, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D4 Archegetes, T.

name: Archilocheion
description: A sanctuary dedicated to the Greek lyric poet Archilochus, known as the Archilocheion, was established on Paros during the third century BCE. It is attested textually in both literature and inscriptions; however, the precise location of the sanctuary has not been identified. During the 19th and 20th centuries, some scholars argued for it to be placed at the site of the “Three Churches” (Tris Ekklisies) because inscriptions mentioning Archilochos were found reused in remains of the 6th century Christian basilica over which the later churches had been built. Other scholars (e.g., Kontoleon in PECS) have vigorously rejected this view. Foss (BAtlDir) cites Berranger in tentatively placing the Archilocheion further east at the modern village of Elitas.

name: Arcia di Niscemi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 E4 Arcia di Niscemi

name: Ardettos
description: A site to the southeast of Athens, where jurors swore their oath, named for a hero Ardettes.

name: Area archeologica di Colle della Noce
description: The ancient temple in the sacred area of Colle della Noce dates to the fifth and fourth century BCE.

name: Ares, Aphrodite, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 E2 Ares, Aphrodite, T.

name: Arian Baptistry
description: Baptistry built by Theodoric the Great in the late fifth or early sixth century AD

name: Aron
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H4 Aron

name: Arsameia pros Nymphaio
description: An ancient fortified city on high ground on the south bank of the river Nymphaios (modern Kâhta Çayı) south of the modern village of Kocahisar (also known as Eski Kahta) in Turkey.

name: Arteda Civitate
description: An early medieval settlement in Spain (9th to 13th centuries CE) listed in contemporary Latin documents. This settlement is now identified with a second phase of occupation of an archaeological site known as El Forau de la Tuta near modern Arieda in Zaragosa province where a previously unknown Roman settlement has also been discovered. In addition to archaeological finds in situ, spolia from both the Roman and medieval phases have been identified in the more recent Ermita de San Pedro.

name: Artemis Diktynnaia, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D4 Artemis Diktynnaia, T.

name: Artemis Elaphebolia, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D3 Artemis Elaphebolia, T.

name: Artemis Hegemone, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 B3 Artemis Hegemone, T.

name: Artemis Laphria Sanctuary (Kalydon)
description: This sanctuary is believed to have been active since at least the Geometric period, possibly on the site of a Mycenaean settlement. Ceramic evidence indicates continuous activity at least through the Roman period.

name: Artemis Parthenos, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 D3 Artemis Parthenos, T.

name: Artemis Tauropolos temple
description: Near Artemida/Loutsa in modern Attiki, the archaeological remains of a temple in the Doric order were first identified in 1956. Excavators identified the sanctuary as belonging to Artemis Tauropolos. The compilers of the Barrington Atlas, in keeping with the discussion offered in PECS by C.W.J. Eliot, associate the temple site with the deme of Halai Araphenides.

name: Artemis Tauropolos, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 C2 Artemis Tauropolos, T.

name: Artemis Thermia, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C3 Artemis Thermia, T.

name: Artemision
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 A3 Artemision

name: Artemision
description: The possible site of the temple of Artemis Proseoa beneath the Ag. Georgios chapel, Artemision (Kourbatsi) Euboea.

name: Arthur's O'On
description: A circular Roman temple that stood on the river Carron until it was destroyed in 1743.

name: Arx of Cosa
description: The citadel of the ancient city and location of its Capitolium.

name: Asabaa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E2 Asabaa

name: Asbamaion
description: A sanctuary sacred to Zeus (Jupiter) Asbamaeus that was located in Cappadocia. According to Ammianus Marcellinus, a sacred spring could be seen at the sanctuary. Apollonius of Tyana was said to have been born here.

name: Asea Vigla
description: A Doric temple dated to the sixth century BCE.

name: Ash Altar of Zeus Lykaios
description: The ash altar of Zeus Lykaios on the southern peak of Mt. Lykaion.

name: Asklepeion of Ancient Trichoneion
description: An excavated sanctuary sacred to Asklepios located at Gavalou in Aitoloakarnania West Greece.

name: Asklepieion
description: The Asklepieion at Pergamon is located 3 km south of the Acropolis.

name: Asklepieion (Kos)
description: A major, ancient sanctuary of Asklepios located on the Aegean island of Kos.

name: Asklepieion and Lerna
description: A sanctuary complex on the northern margins of ancient Corinth near the theater.

name: Asklepieion at Eleusis
description: A sanctuary of Asklepios at Eleusis, established in the late 5th century BC and active through the Imperial era. It is known from finds of inscriptions and sculpture to have been located somewhere north of the sanctuary of Demeter and Kore, but the exact site is lost.

name: Asklepieion at Zea
description: A sanctuary of Asklepios above Zea harbour in Piraeus, founded in the late 5th century BC and active through the Imperial period. Sections of the sanctuary were explored archaeologically in the 1880s and 2000s.

name: Asklepieion of Messene
description: The Asklepieion in Ancient Messene is a sanctuary of Asklepios, the Greek god of healing, constructed in 214 BC. The complex includes civic as well as sacred buildings

name: Asklepieion of Pheneos
description: The Asklepieion of Pheneos dates to the later second century BCE. It was identified via excavation in the late 1950s.

name: Asklepieion of Trikka
description: The Asklepieion of Trikka was said to be the oldest such sanctuary in Greece.

name: Asklepieion, Thesmophorion?, Apollo, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 A3 Asklepieion, Thesmophorion?, Apollo, T.

name: Asklepios, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 B4 Asklepios, T.

name: Aspripetra
description: A cavernous grotto on the island of Kos that seems to have served as a place of worship in ancient times. Pan and the Nymphs were venerated there in Archaic Greek times. The grotto's ancient name is unknown.

name: Atessa/Porcari Sanctuary
description: A small Italic sanctuary, possibly of Vejovis, in a modern area known then as Passo Porcari (between Monte Marcone and Saletti) near Atessa in Italy's Chieti province. It was excavated in the 1970s and is memorialized by the modern street name "Via Veiove". Rescue excavations of purportedly medieval finds were carried out in the immediate vicinity between 2017 and 2020 ahead of a road modification.

name: Athena Itonia?, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D4 Athena Itonia?, T.

name: Athena Kranaia, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D3 Athena Kranaia, T.

name: Athena Poliouchos, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 A3 Athena Poliouchos, T.

name: Athena, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 B3 Athena, T.

name: Athena, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 F2 Athena, T.

name: Athena, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 C3 Athena, T.

name: Athena, Zeus?, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 A5 Athena, Zeus?, T.

name: Athribis/Triph(i)eion
description: Athribis/Triph(i)eion in Upper Egypt, located outside modern Sohag, was the site of a temple built for the goddess Repyt (Triphis) in the Ptolemaic period.

name: Athroula/Laththa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 4 B2 Athroula/Laththa

name: Auguratorium
description: An augural area on the Palatine Hill mentioned in the Regionary Catalogues for Regio X.

name: Augusteum at Carthago Nova/Col. Urbs Iulia
description: A temple dedicated to the imperial cult during the reign of Augustus.

name: Augusteum, Antioch in Pisidia
description: The Augusteum, or Sebasteion, at Antioch in Pisidia was a monumental architectural complex dedicated to the Roman Imperial cult. Its archaeological remains were first excavated in 1913. Approximately 60 fragments of the Res Gestae Divi Augusti were discovered at the site.

name: Ayios Filon
description: A ruined fifth-century CE basilica on the Karpass peninsula of Cyprus.

name: Ayios Photios
description: Caches of sculpture discovered in the 19th century by Luigi di Cesnola provide evidence for a cult of a male deity in the area of the modern Ayios Photios church near Athienou on Cyprus.

name: Ayn-Manawir Temple
description: Temple dedicated to Osiris located 3km west of the modern village of Dush.

name: Baal-Hamman, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 C2 Baal-Hamman, T.

name: Bachkovo Monastery
description: Monastery in southern Bulgaria founded in 1083 CE.

name: Bad Bertrich
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 H2 Bad Bertrich

name: Bağin
description: Village where fragments of a Urartian stele have been found (one in a church, one in a fortress).

name: Bailleul-sur-Thérain
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Bailleul-sur-Thérain

name: Baitokaike
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 B4 Baitokaike

name: Bakcheion
description: A sanctuary to the west of the Acropolis of Athens, which was the clubhouse for a private religious association called the Iobakchoi, in the 2nd century AD.

name: Baldersdorf
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 F3 Baldersdorf

name: Balmarcodes, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C2 Balmarcodes, T.

name: Balsignano
description: A fortified Medieval site located in Puglia.

name: Banteay Srei
description: Buddhist temple at Angkor, located approximately 20 km northeast of the main temple complex. Dedicated to Shiva, the temple was constructed during the 10th century CE during the reigns of Rajendravarman II and Jayavarman V.

name: Baptistery of Butrint
description: The Baptistery of Butrint is a round structure, with a diameter of 14.5 meters. It was built in the sixth century A.D. and was excavated in May 1928 by Luigi Maria Ugolini.

name: Baptistry of Neon
description: An octagonal brick structure erected by Bishop Ursus (late fourth or early fifth century AD) as part of a Basilica (destroyed in 1734). Bishop Neon completed the baptistry at the end of the fifth century AD.

name: Baqirha
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 D4 Baqirha

name: Barbar Temples
description: Archaeological site in northern Bahrain associated with the Dilmun culture. Three successive temples were built on the site during the 3rd-2nd millennia BCE.

name: Bard-i Nishande
description: Bard-i Nishande is the site of a ruined ancient landscape consisting of multiple complexes. According to K. Schippmann, these complexes include a ruler's residence or "castle", a terraced platform used for ritual activity, and a lower town.

name: Barlaamus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 A2 Barlaamus

name: Baron-sur-Odon
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 7 F2 Baron-sur-Odon

name: Barzan (Gallo-Roman Site)
description: Site of a Gallo-Roman sanctuary and subsequent city of the Roman imperial period, the "Site gallo-romain de Barzan" is spread across the modern French communes of Barzan, Talmont-sur-Gironde and Arces-sur-Gironde in the department of Charente-Maritime. BAtlas equated it with ancient Tannum/Tamnum, but alternatives continue to be offered.

name: Basilica and convent of S. Ippolito
description: An important paleochristian complex located in the area of Portus.

name: Basilica Benedettina di S. Michele Arcangelo
description: An abbey church located in Capua on the western slopes of Monte Tifata.

name: Basílica Casa Herrera
description: The Casa Herrera Basilica is part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Archaeological Ensemble of Mérida". The paleochristian basilica was built in either the late fifth or early sixth century CE.

name: Basilica Damous el-Karita
description: A Christian basilica of the Byzantine period located in Carthage.

name: Basílica de Santa Eulalia
description: The Basílica de Santa Eulalia is a paleochristian site that is included as part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Archaeological Ensemble of Mérida". The early Christian features were superimposed upon previous, preexisting Roman features.

name: Basílica de Son Peretó
description: A Paleochristian site dating to the fifth century A.D.

name: Basílica des Fornàs de Torelló
description: Basílica des Fornàs de Torelló, a sixth-century CE paleochristian basilica with mosaic pavements located in Mahon, Minorca.

name: Basilica di San Marco
description: The ruins of a Christian basilica located near the Via Ardeatina on the grounds of the Calixtian complex have been connected with the Basilica of Saint Mark. The site was discovered accidentally in 1991 and is associated with the Catacomb of Balbina.

name: Basilica di Sant'Agnese fuori le mura
description: Pope Honorius I commissions the Basilica of Saint Agnes in the seventh century, replacing the earlier Constantinian structure.

name: Basilica di Sant'Anastasia al Palatino
description: The Basilica of Sant'Anastasia al Palatino was founded ca. 325 CE on the slopes of the Palatine. It is now a minor basilica of the Roman Catholic church. It is the national church for the Peruvian community of Rome and the Indian community of the Syro-Malabar rite. The original basilica was adjacent to the Circus Maximus and came to be dedicated to Saint Anastasia of Sirmium (281-304 CE.), a Roman woman martyred under Diocletian. In the acts of the synod of 499, the church is listed as the titulus Anastasiae.

name: Basilica di Santa Balbina all'Aventino
description: The basilica of Santa Balbina is a paleo-Christian basilica in Rome, located on the "little Aventine".

name: Basilica di Santa Francesca Romana
description: The Basilica of Santa Francesca Romana, also known as Santa Maria Nova, dates originally from the ninth century when the basilica was erected on the site of a preexisting oratory during the papacy of Paul I.

name: Basilica di Santa Sinforosa
description: A minor Paleochristian basilica located in Guidonia Montecelio that was dedicated to the patron saint of Tivoli.

name: Basilica di Santo Stefano in via Latina
description: The now-ruined basilica of Santo Stefano in Via Latina was a fifth-century CE early Christian basilica. It is now located within the confines of the archaeological park of the Via Latina tombs.

name: Basilica Hilariana
description: A basilica erected on the Caelian Hill in Rome under the patronage of Manius Publicus Hilarus and connected to the cult of Cybele and Attis.

name: Basilica Manastirine
description: An important extra-mural Christian site at Solina. The site includes the basilica and the cemetery of Manastirine, associated with a Christian martyr.

name: Basilica of Constantine (Ostia)
description: The presence of a basilica created with funds donated by Constantine I in region 5 of ancient Ostia was known from textual sources and then confirmed via geophysical prospection and, ultimately, excavation.

name: Basilica of Cresconius
description: The Basilica of Cresconius is a church with a nave-and-side-aisle plan located at Cuicul (Djémila), Numidia, Algeria.

name: Basilica of Iomnium
description: The basilica of Iomnium (Tigzirt) likely dates to the fifth and sixth centuries CE.

name: Basilica of Lechaion
description: The basilica of Lechaion was dedicated to Leonides, the bishop of Athens. It is one of the most important early Byzantine monuments in the Peloponnese.

name: Basilica of Saint Augustine in Campo Marzio
description: The Basilica of Saint Augustine of Hippo in Camp Martius is a titular minor basilica of the Roman Catholic church located in the Campus Martius of Rome. The basilica was conceived in 1286 and the current basilica was completed in 1483.

name: Basilica of Saint-Pierre-aux-Nonnains
description: A Christian basilica that incorporates the remains of a fourth-century AD Roman gymnasium.

name: Basilica of San Clemente al Laterano
description: The eleventh-century Basilica of San Clemente al Laterano is a Roman Catholic basilica dedicated to Clement I. It sits atop a three-tiered series of architectural remains stretching back to the Republican period. The earliest phase is represented by the foundations of a building destroyed by the fire of A.D. 64.

name: Basilica of Sant'Apollinare in Classe
description: A basilica begun in the sixth century AD by Bishop Ursicinus with financial support from the Greek banker Iulianus Argentarius.

name: Basilica of Santa Maria in Trastevere
description: The Basilica di Santa Maria in Trastevere is a titular minor basilica located in the Trastevere district of Rome, Italy.

name: Basilica of Santa Pudenziana
description: A palaeochristian church dedicated to St. Pudentiana located in Rome and, perhaps, the earliest site of Christian worship in the city. The church is located along the Via Urbana, which follows the course of the ancient vicus Patricius.

name: Basilica of St. Francis of Assisi
description: A papal basilica begun ca. AD 1228.

name: Basilica of St. John
description: The Basilica of St. John the Evangelist is located in Ephesus on the Ayasuluk Hill. It was built between 548 and 565 CE.

name: Basilica of St. Titus
description: A Christian basilica of the mid-sixth to seventh centuries.

name: Basilica of the Sacred Heart (Paray-le-Monial)
description: 12th-century church in Paray-le-Monial, France. This site was a popular pilgrimage site and is an important example of Cluniac architecture.

name: Basilica of Thyrsos
description: A fifth or sixth century AD (Early Christian) triple-aisled basilica including mosaic floors.

name: Bassai
description: The Temple of Apollo Epikourios (often Latinized as Epicurius) at Bassae is an innovative mid-5th century B.C. Doric temple in Arcadia. The temple preserves the oldest Corinthian capital yet found. It was added to the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1986.

name: Bath-Gymnasium Complex at Sardis
description: Bath/gymnasium complex containing a palaestra and a synagogue that was developed in the 3rd century CE.

name: Bau/Pboou
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 G4 Bau/Pboou

name: Bavian/Khinnis
description: Just a few kilometers upstream of the city, the 7th-century B.C. Assyrian king Sennacherib had an architectural complex built and several stele-like panels (with accompanying inscriptions) carved into a nearby cliff face. The site may have served as a summer residence for the Assyrian king and may have been used as a headquarters to supervise the construction of a vast network of canals and aqueducts that was created specifically for providing water to Nineveh and the Assyrian heartland. The "Khinnis Reliefs" were included on the 2014 World Monuments Watch to increase awareness of cultural heritage in the Khinnis/Bavian area.

name: Bayon at Angkor Thom
description: A temple in the Khmer capital of Angkor Thom, constructed in the late 12th or early 13th century CE.

name: Beaulieu
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 B3 Beaulieu

name: Beit el-Wali
description: Beit el-Wali, meaning "House of the Holy Man", was a site in Nubia where Ramesses II built a temple sacred to Amun-Re, Re-Horakhti, Khnum and Anuket. The temple was relocated in the 1960s.

name: Belbèze
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G2 Belbèze

name: Bell-Loc d’Aro
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 H4 Bell-Loc d’Aro

name: Belmonte
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 E3 Belmonte

name: Belvedere Temple, Orvieto
description: A fifth century B.C. temple that was likely sacred to Tinia.

name: Beni Guedal
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 C2 Beni Guedal

name: Bertelles
description: A "fortified basilica" in Cyrenaica, probably dating to the 6th century CE and associated with nearby farmsteads and tombs, the latter dating to the Roman period, but reused in late antiquity.

name: Beşdut
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 86 E3 Beşdut

name: Beth Alpha
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 B4 Beth Alpha

name: Bethabara
description: Bethabara, in modern Jordan, is an ancient site connected with John the Baptist. Bethabara is also attested on the sixth century A.D. "Madaba Map".

name: Biclaro?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F4 Biclaro?

name: Bidoni
description: A monumental sanctuary (26 x 20 m) dedicated to Iuppiter.

name: Binbirkilise
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 66 C2 Binbirkilise

name: Bir Abu Swera
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D5 Bir Abu Swera

name: Bir Ftouha basilica
description: An Early Christian pilgrimage complex and baptistry at Carthage.

name: Bir Nesma
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F3 Bir Nesma

name: Bishop Genesius Church
description: The Bishop Genesius Church at Jerash (Jordan) may be the earliest Christian church on the site. The church was built in the early seventh century, reusing a preexisting Roman temple.

name: Bitalemi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 E4 Bitalemi

name: Bıçkıcı Kalesi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 66 A4 Bıçkıcı Kalesi

name: Blatades Monastery
description: A fourteenth century monastery.

name: Boades
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G4 Boades

name: Bona Dea Subsaxana, Aedes
description: An ancient sanctuary of the Aventine Hill in Rome.

name: Bostra/Col. Nova Traiana Alexandriana
description: The ancient city of Bostra (modern Busra in Syria), where extensive ancient Nabataean, Roman, Byzantine and Umayyad structures still visible. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1980.

name: Bou Kisnaden
description: This site, discussed in scholarly literature in the 20th century, seems from satellite imagery to have been impinged or destroyed in the construction of the Rocade Tipaza/Autoroute Tipaza - Cherchel in the early years of the 21st century. It seems to have been a villa with a central courtyard and primary entrance facing east, with a row of rooms with arched openings overlooking the wadi to the north. Vats and presses, possibly for processing grapes for wine, were discovered. Anselmino hypothesized that another, unexcavated structure immediately to the east was a church.

name: Bouleuterion of Onchestos
description: The Bouleuterion of Onchestos has been identified adjacent to the Temple of Poseidon.

name: Bracquemont
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B3 Bracquemont

name: Brampton Old Church
description: The Brampton Old Church stands on the site of a Roman fort located 13 km northeast of Carlisle and 0.8 km south of the Stanegate.

name: Braq
description: Khirbet Braq is the modern name of a ruined Nabatean sanctuary at Ayn Braq in Jordan, a spring that, in antiquity, was channeled through an aqueduct to supply ancient Petra. The heavily looted site is located just south of modern Wadi Musa, on the west side of the modern road to Taybeh.

name: Brauron/sanctuary of Artemis Brauronia
description: Brauron was a sacred site in Attica dedicated to Artemis Brauronia. The sanctuary was active from the Middle Bronze Age period, although a gap in activity stretches from the Final Bronze Age to the eighth century B.C.

name: Brean Down
description: Brean Down is the site of a Romano-British temple that was built ca. A.D. 340 and destroyed about 50 years later. The site was excavated between 1957-8.

name: Breviglieri
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F2 Breviglieri

name: Brides-les-Bains
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 H3 Brides-les-Bains

name: Brigstock
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G2 Brigstock

name: Brihadishwara Temple
description: 11th century Hndu temple Tanjuvar dedicated to Shiva with smaller shrines around it.

name: Brikinniai?
description: A fortified ancient settlement near modern Lentini. It has been associated with the area of Monte San Basilio.

name: Britaniensis/Britonensis Ecclesia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 D1 Britaniensis/Britonensis Ecclesia

name: Brittablo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 H4 Brittablo

name: Bu Darag
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 C1 Bu Darag

name: Building B
description: An apsidal structure on the Acropolis of Athens, built in the late 6th-century BC, destroyed in the Persian Sack of 480 BC, and perhaps repaired in the first half of the 5th century. It was probably located on the site of the northwest wing of the later Propylaia, which would have superseded it in 437 BC.

name: Bully
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D2 Bully

name: Burgstall am Schlern cult site
description: A "Brandopferplatz" (Alpine burnt-offerings cult place) located at the so-called Burgstall, the topographically distinctive northern peak of the Schlern, a mountain in the South Tirolian Dolomites in modern Italy.

name: Burj al-Qa'im
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 91 E3 Burj al-Qa'im

name: Buscemi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 F4 Buscemi

name: Büyük Çelebi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 A4 Büyük Çelebi

name: Bziza
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 A5 Bziza

name: Caere
description: Caere (also known as Cisra or Cerveteri) was a major Etruscan polis located ca. 50 km north-northwest of Rome in southern Etruria.

name: Caesarea Mithraeum
description: The Mithraeum at Caesarea is only one of two excavated from the eastern half of the Roman empire.

name: Caesareum of Alexandria
description: Hellenistic temple constructed by Cleopatra VII and dedicated to Julius Caesar, and later rededicated by Augustus to himself. In 13 BCE Augustus relocated two obelisks from Heliopolis to the site. The temple was converted to a Christian church in the mid-4th century.

name: Caesareum of Palmyra
description: The complex, identified as the Caesareum of Palmyra, was converted into a mosque during the Umayyad period.

name: Calalzo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 E3 Calalzo

name: Camarina
description: Camarina was founded by Syracuse in 599 B.C., but subsequently destroyed in 552 B.C. The city was re-founded by Gela in 461 B.C. Carthage razed the site in 405 B.C., but Timoleon restored it in 339 B.C. From the late third century B.C. the town fell under Roman administration.

name: Camp celtique de la Bure
description: A Gallo-Roman settlement of a commercial nature located on the Meurthe river. Archaeological evidence for earthworks, as well as ritual activity, has been discovered. The Gallo-Roman ramparts, in a later phase, make use of marble sculpture as spolia.

name: Camp-Ferrus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G1 Camp-Ferrus

name: Campochiaro
description: An Italic sanctuary sacred to Hercules located in località Civitella in the comune of Campochiaro. The site is to be connected with the Pentrian Samnites.

name: Campoverde
description: Campoverde (Lazio) lies some 60 km south of Rome. In 1977-1978 a quantity of votive material was recovered from a body of water known as the "Laghetto del Monsignore". This spring-fed body of water was interpreted as an open-air votive depository.

name: Can Modolell
description: An ancient rural sanctuary of Hispania (Cabrera de Mar, Barcelona).

name: Cancho Roano
description: A pre-Roman sanctuary of the Iberian peninsula.

name: Cantobennum, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I3 Cantobennum, Mon.

name: Capaccio
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 B4 Capaccio

name: Capitolium
description: The so-called Capitolium of Aquinum is found in the northwest part of the site. It may predate Roman colonization, and it is perhaps sacred to Iuno Regina.

name: Capitolium at Mactaris
description: The Capitolium temple at Mactaris likely dates to the reign of Marcus Aurelius.

name: Capitolium at Thugga
description: The Capitolium at Thugga dates to the middle of the second century and carries epigraphic dedications to Lucius Verus and Marcus Aurelius dating to the late 160s.

name: Capitolium of Brixia
description: The Capitolium of Brixia was built in A.D. 73.

name: Capitolium of Cosa
description: The so-called Capitolium temple of the Latin colony of Cosa.

name: Capitolium of Sala
description: Tetrastyle Corinthian temple located in the Roman forum at Sala (modern Chellah) dating from the early Hadrianic period (c.120 CE).

name: Capitolium of Sarsina
description: The remains of a first-century BCE temple podium discovered at Sarsina have been identified as the Roman settlement's Capitolium.

name: Capitolium of Terracina
description: A Tuscanic podium temple that is often referred to as being of the Capitolium type was built at Terracina in the middle of the first century BCE.

name: Capitolium of Verona
description: Excavations have determined the location of the capitolium of Roman Verona, part of an extensive, terraced complex that underpins medieval and later structures of the city. The capitolium itself stretches from the northern end of the modern Piazza Erbe to the via Emilei, lying beneath the Palazzo Maffei, church of San Benedetto, Palazzo Malaspina and surrounding structures.

name: Capitolium Vetus
description: The Capitolium Vetus was located on the Quirinal Hill and was an earlier shrine to the triad of Iuppiter, Juno, and Minerva than the famous Capitoline Temple. It was likely perched on the northern edge of the Quirinal.

name: Capo Cimiti
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 46 F4 Capo Cimiti

name: Capodifiume
description: Remains of a fourth-century B.C. sanctuary situated at a spring known as Caput aquae, from which the modern toponym is most likely derived.

name: Cappella di Sant'Antonino (Finale Ligure)
description: A twelfth-century chapel located near the Castrum Perticae. The site is located in the municipal territory of modern Finale Ligure in Italy's Ligurian Alps.

name: Carovilli
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 F2 Carovilli

name: Casalbore
description: At modern Casalbore, Italy, archaeological evidence includes a significant temple structure and associated material from the Samnite and Roman periods.

name: Casalicchio
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 D4 Casalicchio

name: Castelfusano Paleochristian basilica
description: Situated along the via Severiana are the remains of an anonymous Paleochristian basilica that was in use between the second and the seventh centuries AD.

name: Castellar
description: A Gallic oppidum and sanctuary to the god Dexiva in the Vaucluse départment.

name: Castellnou de Basella
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G4 Castellnou de Basella

name: Castellu di Cucuruzzu
description: Castellu di Cucuruzzu is a Torrean archaeological site in Corsica.

name: Castelnau-Montratier
description: A Gallo-Roman site located in the Barguelonne valley that covers some 4,000 square meters. Excavation (1962-1964) suggested the site was likely a rural sanctuary that included a theater and baths.

name: Castiglione del Lago
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 C2 Castiglione del Lago

name: Castle Acre Priory
description: A priory founded in 1089 by William de Warenne and consecrated sometime between 1146 and 1148. Home of the first Cluniac order in England.

name: Castrum Caetani
description: A Medieval fortification that incorporates and repurposes the Roman mausoleum of Caecilia Metella.

name: Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta
description: The Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta on the island of Torcello is a Roman Catholic basilica initially founded in 639. The second basilica was begun in 1008.

name: Cathedral of St. James, Jerusalem
description: A 12th-century Armenian church in Jerusalem, the principal church of the Armenian Patriarchate of Jerusalem.

name: Cathedral of the Holy Cross at Aghtamar
description: Armenian church built on the island of Aghtamar by King Gagik I Artsruni between 915-921 CE.

name: Çatıören Church
description: Byzantine church remains at Çatıören in Rough Cilicia

name: Cattedrale di Santa Maria di Amiternum
description: The ruins of the cathedral of the fifth century are located in an area of the archaeological site of Amiternum that was occupied in the second and third centuries CE.

name: Cave 1 at Udayagiri
description: The southernmost cave at Udayagiri, dedicated to an unidentified god.

name: Cave 13 at Udayagiri
description: Cave 13 at Udayagiri, known as the Nārāyaṇa cave, contains a large relief panel of Vishnu's Nārāyaṇa avatar.

name: Cave 19 at Udayagiri
description: The largest cave temple at Udayagiri, known as the Amrita or Viṣṇupada cave.

name: Cave 5 at Udayagiri
description: Cave 5 at Udayagiri is dedicated to Vishnu and contains a monumental relief panel of Vishnu's Varaha avatar.

name: Cave of Apollo
description: The Cave of Apollo was a shrine on the north slope of the Acropolis, dedicated to Apollo Hypakraios / hypo Akrais / hypo Makrais ("Apollo under the High Rocks" / "under the Long Rocks"). According to Euripides' Ion, it was the birthplace of Ion, progenitor of the Ionians.

name: Cave of Pan and the Nymphs
description: A small cave, located northeast of Vari in Attica, Greece, that was used as a shrine dedicated to Apollo, Pan, and the Nymphs from the sixth to the second centuries BC.

name: Cave of Saint Peter
description: A cave in the outskirts of Antioch that was converted into a church.

name: Cengerli
description: A likely Roman fort and possible Urartian fire temple at the modern village of Cengerli in the Refahiye district of Erzincan province, Turkey.

name: Central Building at Kalhu
description: Near the middle of the citadel of Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud), the ninth-century-BC Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) constructed a building with a grand, south-facing entrance. This building, constructed east of the southeast corner of the Northwest Palace, might have been one of the temples built by Ashurnasirpal II when he made Kalhu the administrative capital of the Assyrian Empire. Alternatively, the Central Building might have served as a palace.

name: Ceres et Faustina (temple)
description: The Roman temple dedicated to Ceres and Faustina received patronage from Herodes Atticus. Its remains were eventually repurposed in the construction of the church of Sant'Urbano.

name: Cerreto Sannita
description: A Samnite temple dedicated to Flora.

name: Cerro de los Santos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 27 D3 Cerro de los Santos

name: Chafagi Aamer
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F3 Chafagi Aamer

name: Chahar Deh
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 A3 Chahar Deh

name: Chale Ghar 1
description: Bronze Age copper mine reused as a sanctuary or ritual site from the 8th century BCE-8th century CE, located near the modern village of Veshnaveh, Iran.

name: Chalencon
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D4 Chalencon

name: Chalia
description: Theopompus mentions Chalia as a town of Boeotia. It is also attested epigraphically and may have been a member of the Boeotian league. Fossey locates the city in the vicinity of the church of Agios Minas in modern Drosia. Archaeological finds in the area indicate human activity from as early as the Bronze Age.

name: Champlieu
description: A sanctuary located at the boundary of the civitas of the Suessiones and that of the Silvanectes.

name: Chanac
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 A5 Chanac

name: Chanceaux-sur-Choisille
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 F1 Chanceaux-sur-Choisille

name: Changiyeh
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B3 Changiyeh

name: Chapel of Hakor
description: Chapel for the Sacred Barque of Amun-Ra in Karnak, established by the Egyptian pharaoh Hakor of the Twenty-ninth dynasty who reigned from 392/1 to 379/8 BCE.

name: Chapel of Wadjmose
description: Chapel dedicated to the prince Wadjmose, son of Pharaoh Thutmose I.

name: Chapelle Sainte-Agathe
description: The chapelle Sainte-Agathe was originally a Roman-period thermal complex that was converted to a Christian church by the seventh century.

name: Chariton
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 G2 Chariton

name: Château de Chatillon
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G1 Château de Chatillon

name: Château-Bas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 15 E2 Château-Bas

name: Châteaubleau
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 D4 Châteaubleau

name: Châtelard de Lardiers
description: A Celto-Ligurian oppidum and sanctuary.

name: Chettaba
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 31 E4 Chettaba

name: Chiesa di San Paolo di Peltuinum
description: The church of San Paolo at Peltuinum likely dates to the seventh or eighth century CE.

name: Chiesa di Sant'Ilario a Port' Aurea
description: A sixth-, seventh-, and eighth-century Christian church located near the Port'Aurea of Benevento was built during the Longobard period. The church was built atop Imperial remains and was an apsidal hall.

name: Chiesa di Santa Pudenziana, Narni
description: The church of Santa Pudenziana near Narni, Italy, dates from the ninth to eleventh centuries CE and reuses spoliated architectural elements from a Roman villa.

name: Chiesa Ipogea di San Nicola
description: A poorly understood paleochristian site located on the grounds of the Inviolata park in the comune of Guidonia-Montecelio in the greater metropolitan area of Rome. The cave site may originally have functioned as a mithraeum.

name: Chordiraza?/Baisampse?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 G3 Chordiraza?/Baisampse?

name: Choziba
description: A desert monastery, founded in the fifth century CE, which was located where the 19th-century Greek Orthodox monastery of Deir Mar Jirys now stands on the north cliffs above the Wadi Qelt/Kelt in the West Bank, nearly 5km west from the center of modern Jericho.

name: Christian Basilica, Ostia
description: The so-called Christian Basilica, located in Regio III of ancient Ostia, was discovered by Guido Calza in 1939. The structure likely dates to the fourth and fifth centuries CE. The interpretation of the structure and its remains has proven difficult, resulting in numerous theories that are often at odds with one another. One possibility is that the complex functioned as a xenodochium (a guest house for pilgrims).

name: Christian House Church
description: Church adapted from a private house building in Dura-Europos.

name: Chrysas, T.
description: According to Cicero, the shrine of Chrysas (the god shares a name with the nearby river) was located along the road from Assorus to Henna. A cult image in marble, described as being "beautiful", was to be found there and was ultimately spared from the depredations of Caius Verres.

name: Church and early Christian necropolis at Vaste
description: An early Christian complex that included a cult building with several construction phases and a vast cemetery.

name: Church at Shams el-Din
description: The church, well visible in Google Earth, is in the SW sector of the archaeological area

name: Church at Trimithis
description: A structure at the city of Trimithis (Amheida) in the Dakhleh Oasis, identified as a church and dated to the 4th century CE.

name: Church in Sidi Khiribech
description: Ruins of a church likely built around the fifth century AD, on the summit of the low hill of Sidi Khiribech in Berenike. The character of the building and its position has suggested to some archaeologists a defensive as well as a religious purpose.

name: Church of Mren
description: 7th-century Armenian church in the Kars region of Turkey. This church is now ruined, but still is the second-largest domed basilica from the 7th-century Caucasus still extant.

name: Church of Panagia Chalkeon
description: An eleventh-century A.D. Byzantine church in Thessaloniki.

name: Church of Prophet Elijah
description: A fourteenth-century AD church in Thessaloniki.

name: Church of Saint Alexander Nevsky, Jerusalem
description: The nineteenth century Russian Orthodox church preserves within it remains of Constantine's original fourth century church of the Holy Sepulchre.

name: Church of Saint Catherine, Thessaloniki
description: A late Byzantine church in Thessalonica.

name: Church of Saint Demetrius
description: A seventh century AD church dedicated to the patron saint of Thessalonica; the present basilica stands on the site of a precursor with phases beginning in the fourth century AD.

name: Church of Saint Eusebius (Ronciglione)
description: A paleochristian sanctuary located along the Via Francigena.

name: Church of Saint Gregory of Tigran
description: Armenian church in Ani, in modern Eastern Turkey, completed in 1215.

name: Church of Saint Martin at Moissac
description: A Christian church built, at least in part, atop the ruins of a Gallo-Roman building that include a hypocaust.

name: Church of Saint Panteleimon
description: The Church of Saint Panteleimon (Thessaloniki) is a late Byzantine church.

name: Church of Saint Sophia
description: The Church of Saint Sophia, also referred to as the Old Bishopric, is an Eastern Orthodox church located in Nesebar, eastern Bulgaria. The church originally dates to the sixth century.

name: Church of San Primitivo
description: The so-called Church of San Primitivo in Monte Compatri. The church's bell tower was converted to a defensive tower around 1200.

name: Church of Santa Fosca
description: The Church of Santa Fosca on the island of Torcello is a Romanesque church dating initially to the ninth century CE.

name: Church of St. Demetrios Katephores
description: A church in Athens, excavated and demolished between 1859 and 1861, which was built on top of a section of the post-Herulian wall incorporating several hundred inscriptions and sculptures, mostly from the Diogeneion gymnasium.

name: Church of St. Etchmiadzin at Soradir
description: Sixth-century Armenian church in the town of Soradir (modern Yanal, Turkey).

name: Church of St. George
description: One of a group of eleven rock-hewn monolithic churches located in Lalibela, a city in the Amhara Region of Ethiopia. The church likely dates to the twelfth or thirteenth century.

name: Church of St. Gião
description: A Paleochristian sanctuary in Portugal.

name: Church of St. Nicholas Orphanos
description: A fourteenth-century A.D. church in Thessalonica.

name: Church of St. Peter
description: A twelfth-century AD Christian church was built atop the remains of the Roman temple of Apollo.

name: Church of the Acheiropoietos
description: A fifth century A.D. Byzantine church.

name: Church of the Annunziatella
description: The church of the Annunziatella is a Catholic church located in Rome's Ardeatino district. It is situated between the via Ardeatina and via di Grotta Perfetta. The church was established in the thirteenth century, as an inscription of Pope Honorius III attests.

name: Church of the Archangels at Sige
description: Church in the village of Sige (modern Kumyaka) on the southern shore of the Sea of Marmara. Constructed under Constantine VI in 780, the structure was restored twice, in 1448 and 1819. It remained in continuous use until it was ultimately abandoned in 1922.

name: Church of the Holy Apostles
description: A fourteenth century church at Thessalonica.

name: Church of the Holy Apostles (Patmos)
description: 17th-century church behind the Monastery of Saint John the Theologian in Patmos.

name: Church of the Holy Apostles at Constantinople
description: Construction of a church dedicated to the Twelve Apostles at Constantinople began in 330 CE under Constantine the Great, and was completed during the reign of his son Constantius II (331-367). A larger church was established on the same site in 550 by Justinian I. The church was demolished by Mehmed II in 1462, and the Fatih Mosque was constructed on its site.

name: Church of the Nativity
description: A church that many claim marks the birthplace of Jesus Christ is located in Bethlehem, now in the State of Palestine in the West Bank. The original church built on the site was commissioned by Constantine I and dedicated in May 339 CE. The church was destroyed by fire, likely in 529, and replaced by Justinian I.

name: Church of the Saviour, Thessaloniki
description: The Church of the Saviour, Thessaloniki, is a fourteenth-century Byzantine church.

name: Circus Maximus Mithraeum
description: A mithraeum adjacent to the Circus Maximus and facing the carceres of the same was located at the margin of the Forum Boarium and now situated beneath a building in the via dell'Ara Massima. The site was excavated in 1931.

name: City Eleusinion at Athens
description: The City Eleusinion was one of the most important sanctuaries in the religious life of ancient Athens. It was the urban “branch” of the sanctuary of Demeter and Kore at Eleusis in use during the Archaic and Classical periods.

name: Civita di Bagno
description: Civita di Bagno is the modern name for a small settlement that dates back at least to the Roman Late Republican period. Within the modern town, several key monuments are known, such as a podium temple (late 2nd c. BCE?), identified as that of Feronia by an inscription from the reign of Tiberius, a bath complex beneath Palazzo Oliva, and the medieval remains of the cathedral of San Massimo which reuses much ancient material, as well as funerary remains along the ancient road network. On the adjacent Moritola hill is a vast system of terracing, which in its earliest phase dates to the 2nd c.
