BCE, that supported a monumental complex of difficult interpretation, but with an important hydraulic infrastructure. This complex underwent multiple modifications over the centuries; in the 5th c. CE the complex was transformed by the installation of two kilns and a limekiln. By the 6th c. CE Civita di Bagno was the seat of a bishop.

name: Classical Temple at Ai Lia
description: Distyle in antis temple of the fourth century BCE, built from the salvage of the large archaic temple, likely destroyed in the earthquake of 373 BCE. This temple remained in use until the first or second centuries CE.

name: Clepsydra of the Oropos Amphiareion
description: The Oropos Amphiareion clepsydra is a hydraulic inflow clock serving the Sanctuary of Amphiaraus. Situated on the southeast bank of the sacred spring of Amphiaraus, this inflow hydraulic clock dates to the 4th century BCE.

name: Cocullo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E1 Cocullo

name: Coleshill
description: Excavations in the late 1970s revealed evidence for a multi-phase sanctuary. The Iron Age site was of circular plan, while the Romano-British site, of two phases, consisted of a walled enclosure with two shrines.

name: Colle Sparanise
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 G2 Colle Sparanise

name: Colonnes de Riez
description: The remains of a Roman temple of the first century CE.

name: Comblessac
description: A heptagonal Gallo-Roman temple.

name: Compitum
description: A multi-period sanctuary near Anagnia.

name: Coombe Hill
description: Coombe Hill in East Sussex is the site of a Neolithic causeway enclosure built ca. 3200 B.C.

name: Corlier
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 E1 Corlier

name: Corvey
description: The abbey was first founded in 815 by Charlemagne's cousins, Wala and Adelard.

name: Costa Bellene/Ballene (Capo Don?)
description: A Roman mansio on the via Iulia Augusta. An Early Christian complex (basilica, baptistery, cemetery) developed nearby and lasted until the Middle Ages.

name: Costa Murata archaeological area
description: A site with votive activity from the Archaic period and a later Roman house in addition to the basalt-paved Roman road.

name: Coste Guizon
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 15 A1 Coste Guizon

name: Cova de les Dones
description: The cave known as Cova de les Dones is located in the Millares municipality, province of Valencia, Spain. This site shows evidence of human activity across a broad period stretching from the Paleolithic to the time of the Roman Empire.

name: Coventina's Well
description: A shrine to a Celtic water goddess situated near the Roman fort of Brocolitia at present-day Carrawburgh, along Hadrian's Wall.

name: Crepaddosso
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 D3 Crepaddosso

name: Crossroads Enclosure
description: A square sacred enclosure in the northwest corner of the Agora, in front of the Stoa Basileios at the meeting point of the west road and the Panathenaic Way. It was built ca. 430 BC around a sacred rock. It was gradually obscured by the rising ground levels and built over in the 3rd century AD.

name: Crypt and church of San Lussorio
description: The Romanesque church of San Lussorio dates to the early twelfth century.

name: Cueva del Agua
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 D3 Cueva del Agua

name: Cybele sanctuary
description: A temple sacred to Cybele located at Neuss am Rhein.

name: Cybele, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 A2 Cybele, T.

name: Cybele, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 B2 Cybele, T.

name: Cybele, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D3 Cybele, T.

name: Cyriacus, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 C3 Cyriacus, Mon.

name: Dagana
description: An ancient city of southern Sri Lanka. It was characterized by Ptolemy as "sacred to Selene", a description repeated in the Arabic geographic tradition. Its precise location is uncertain: BAtlDir echoes earlier suggestions (see RE) that tentatively identify it with Tangalla, but RE would prefer as "more likely" an identification with the temple complex and associated settlement at Tenavaram (modern Tongra), an identification also suggested by earlier scholars.

name: Danielsberg
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 F3 Danielsberg

name: Daphni Monastery
description: The current Daphni Monastery in Attica dates to the eleventh century, yet its earlier Christian phases belong to the sixth century AD when a Christian site grew atop the abandoned and destroyed sanctuary of Apollo Daphnaios.

name: Darre-ye Barre
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C4 Darre-ye Barre

name: Daventry
description: Roman building interpreted either as a villa or a shrine at Borough Hill hillfort, United Kingdom.

name: Dea Dia (temple)
description: The Arval Brethren cultivated the cult of the Dea Dia. The remains of this Augustan era temple, located at La Tavernaccia restaurant in the Magliana Vecchia neighborhood of Rome, are subterranean and not open to the public.

name: Deir Abou Makar
description: A Coptic Orthodox monastery located in the Wadi El Natrun northwest of Cairo.

name: Deir Abou Menas
description: An ancient Christian pilgrimage site that grew into an active town centered around the monastery complex devoted to Saint Menas who is said to have been buried there. The town reached its peak as the most visited pilgrimage site in Egypt during the sixth century AD.

name: Deir Abu Fam
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 D1 Deir Abu Fam

name: Deir Abu Harmina
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F3 Deir Abu Harmina

name: Deir Abu Hennes
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 D1 Deir Abu Hennes

name: Deir Abu Masis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F4 Deir Abu Masis

name: Deir Abu Matta
description: Late antique settlement, possibly monastic, with substantial remains of a church, mud-brick structures, and tombs.

name: Deir Am-Nasara
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F3 Deir Am-Nasara

name: Deir Amba Baramos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 74 C4 Deir Amba Baramos

name: Deir Amba Bishoi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 74 C4 Deir Amba Bishoi

name: Deir Anba Bakhum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F3 Deir Anba Bakhum

name: Deir Anba Pschoi/Deir Anba Bishai
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 D1 Deir Anba Pschoi/Deir Anba Bishai

name: Deir Antush
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D5 Deir Antush

name: Deir Dronkeh
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E2 Deir Dronkeh

name: Deir el Qal'a
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 G1 Deir el Qal'a

name: Deir el-Adra
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F3 Deir el-Adra

name: Deir el-Bakhit
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 B2 Deir el-Bakhit

name: Deir el-Dik
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 D1 Deir el-Dik

name: Deir el-Kosseir
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 D2 Deir el-Kosseir

name: Deir el-Malek
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 G4 Deir el-Malek

name: Deir el-Muttin
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E2 Deir el-Muttin

name: Deir el-Qattal el-Bizanti
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 71 A3 Deir el-Qattal el-Bizanti

name: Deir el-Shelwit
description: A small temple dedicated to Isis on West bank of the Nile at Luxor. Its ancient name is unknown.

name: Deir el-Shuhada
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F3 Deir el-Shuhada

name: Deir el-Sourian
description: A monastery located in the modern Wadi El Natrun in Egypt, likely founded in the 6th century CE. The ancient church and some of its early decorative program survive.

name: Deir Madoud
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F3 Deir Madoud

name: Deir Magara
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E2 Deir Magara

name: Deir Rumhan
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D5 Deir Rumhan

name: Deir Sombat
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 D1 Deir Sombat

name: Deir Umm Arad
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D5 Deir Umm Arad

name: Deir Umm Butme
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D5 Deir Umm Butme

name: Deirouné
description: An ancient place, located in modern Syria, with remains of a monastic chapel. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011 as part of the "Ancient Villages of Northern Syria."

name: Delion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 A3 Delion

name: Delos Synagogue
description: A complex located near the Stadium Quarter in Delos was identified by André Plassart in 1912. Scholarly opinion on whether or not the complex was, in fact, a synagogue remains mixed. Trümper's analysis (2004) reasserts the likelihood of the structure serving as a synagogue.

name: Delphi
description: The ancient panhellenic sanctuary of Delphi in Greece, seat of the oracle of Apollo.

name: Delphinion
description: A sanctuary of Apollo Delphinios in Athens, which was closely associated with Theseus and served as a law court for cases of justifiable homicide. It is perhaps to be identified with the Classical Doric temple, whose remains are located south of the Olympieion.

name: Delphinion (Miletos)
description: The Hellenistic- and Roman-period sanctuary of Apollo Delphinios is located northeast of the North Market at Miletos.

name: Demeter Sanctuary (Eleusis)
description: The Sanctuary of Demeter at Eleusis in Attica was the site of the celebration of the Eleusinian mysteries, observed from the Archaic period to Late Antiquity.

name: Demeter Temple (Aegae - Yuntdağı)
description: A temple complex at Aegae-Yuntdağı, associated with the cult of Demeter and Kore based on epigraphic evidence, may also have been sacred to other divinities. The site is located to the west of the Scaenae frons of the theater complex.

name: Demeter, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 23 F2 Demeter, T.

name: Demeter, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D3 Demeter, T.

name: Deneuvre
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G4 Deneuvre

name: Dermech basilica
description: A Christian basilica of Byzantine Carthage.

name: Desaignes
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D4 Desaignes

name: Deuilly
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 D4 Deuilly

name: Dharmaraja Cave
description: Modern name of a seventh-century Pallava cave temple in Mahābalipuram.

name: Dharmaraja Ratha
description: Modern name of a seventh-century Pallava rock-cut temple in Mahābalipuram.

name: Diana Tifatina, T.
description: The Fanum Dianae Tifatinae located on the slopes of Mt. Tifata in Campania.

name: Didyma/Branchidai/Ioustinianopolis
description: The ancient sanctuary of Apollo near Miletos (modern Didim, Turkey).

name: Didymoi
description: A place described by Pausanias who indicates that three sanctuaries are to be found here, namely those of Apollo, Poseidon, and Demeter.

name: Diktynnaion
description: A sanctuary located at Menies bay in Chania, Crete, with remains ranging in date from Hellenistic period to Late Antiquity.

name: Dilmun Temple at Saar
description: 20th century BCE Dilmun temple on the highest part of the ancient settlement mound at Saar in modern Bahrain.

name: Dimri
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 C2 Dimri

name: Dionysos Bresagenes, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C3 Dionysos Bresagenes, T.

name: Dionysos, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D3 Dionysos, T.

name: Dionysus Sanctuary
description: An ancient sanctuary located in Maroneia-Sapes Municipality, Greece.

name: Djedars
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 29 G1 Djedars

name: Domburg
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 D1 Domburg

name: Domus and Mitreo delle Pareti Dipinte
description: The complex known as "Domus and Mitreo delle Pareti Dipinte" began its life in the later second century BCE and was remodeled during the time of Augustus. A mithraeum is built in the northwest part of the building in the later second century CE.

name: Domus Cilonis
description: The house of Lucius Fabius Cilo, praefectus urbi in 203, consul in 193 and 204, and a close friend of the emperor Septimius Severus. The house was a gift of the Severans and appears on the Severan marble plan. Its site is now occupied by the basilica of Santa Balbina on the "little Aventine".

name: Donon
description: A sanctuary of the god Mercury in the Vosges Mountains.

name: Doric temple
description: A temple of the Doric order (VIII,7,30-34) located in the so-called Triangular Forum of Pompeii.

name: Doric Temple at Kardaki
description: An Archaic Greek temple at Corfu built in the Doric order ca. 500 B.C.

name: Doric temple at Kaulonia
description: The Doric Temple at Caulonia has been dated ca. 420 B.C.

name: Doric temple of Segesta
description: The Doric temple of Segesta is an incomplete (6 x 14) temple of the Doric order begun in the 420s B.C.

name: Dörpfeld foundations
description: The foundations of a 6th century BC temple on the Acropolis of Athens, dedicated to Athena Polias. It is disputed whether these foundations belong to the same structure as the 6th-century BC architectural fragments known as the Bluebeard temple. At the end of the 6th century, the foundations were reused for another temple of Athena Polias, the Archaios Neos, which was destroyed by the Persians in 480 BC.

name: Doss Trento
description: Doss Trento is a small hill that lies along the course of the Adige River. A Paleochristian basilica was located on the hillside.

name: Dranda
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 G2 Dranda

name: Drion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 C1 Drion

name: Duino Mithraeum
description: A Mithraeum near Duino, in the province of Trieste, north-eastern Italy, that is located within a natural cave.

name: Dún Ailinne
description: An ancient ceremonial site located in County Kildare, Ireland. The circular enclosure once covered ca. 13 ha.

name: Dur-(Kuri)galzu
description: Dur-(Kuri)galzu was founded by the Kassite ruler Kurigalzu ca. 1400 B.C. At its peak the site covered some 225 ha.

name: E-ibbi-Anum
description: The temple of the god Uraš at Dilbat. The Akkadian ceremonial name E-ibbi-Anum means "House, Anu Called It into Being."

name: E(...)gakuga
description: The shrine/seat of the god Ennugi in the Egipar temple at Ur. The name is damaged and the complete Sumerian ceremonial name of the structure is not known.

name: Ea temple (Khorsabad)
description: As part of the palatial complex of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) constructed six temples, including one to the god Ea.

name: Ea-Damkina temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the god Ea and the goddess Damkina. Although its location is not known, the Ea-Damkina temple at Kalhu is thought to have been situated within the Ninurta temple complex, in the northwest corner of the citadel.

name: Eadgigi
description: The shrine/seat of the god Nusku in the Egipar temple at Ur. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eadgigi means "House of the Counsellor."

name: Eamaškuga
description: The temple of the goddess Ningal at Kissik. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eamaškuga means "House, Pure Sheepfold."

name: Eandasaya
description: The temple of the goddess Ištar of the Star, Eandasaya (“House Which Rivals Heaven”), was located in the New City district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of two temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Eanna
description: The temple of the god Anu and the goddess Ištar at Uruk. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eanna means "House of Heaven."

name: Eanšar
description: The shrine in the Egipar temple at Ur. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eanšar means "House of All Heaven."

name: Early Mithraeum
description: First phase of a sacred space dedicated to the worship of the god Mithras at Dura-Europos.

name: Eastern sanctuary
description: An extra-mural sanctuary located at the eastern gate of Gabii

name: Ebabbar
description: The temple of the god Šamaš at Sippar. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ebabbar means "Shining House."

name: Ebabbar
description: The temple of the god Šamaš at Larsa. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ebabbar means "Shining House."

name: Ebursasa
description: The temple of the god Šara, Ebursasa (“House of Beautiful Jars”), was located in the Bāb-Lugalerra district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of three temples in that part of Babylon.

name: ed-Deir
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 E4 ed-Deir

name: Edimgalkalama
description: The temple of the god Ištarān/Anu-rabû at Der. The Sumerian ceremonial name Edimgalkalama means "House, Great Bond of the Land."

name: Eduranna
description: The ziggurat of the god Šamaš at Larsa. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eduranna means "House, Bond of Heaven."

name: Edurkuga
description: The dais of the Igīgū gods, Edurkuga (“House, Pure Abode”), was located in the TE.E (read as either Tê or Kisīri) district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of two daises in that part of Babylon.

name: Egalmah
description: The temple of the goddess Gula (as Ninisinna) at Isin. The Sumerian ceremonial name Egalmah means "Exalted Palace."

name: Egipar
description: The Giparu-House at Ur was the temple of the high-priestess of the moon-god.

name: Egišhurankia
description: The temple of the goddess Bēlet-Ninūa, Egišhurankia (“House of the Ordinances of Heaven and Underworld”), was located in the Bāb-Lugalerra district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of three temples in that part of Babylon. Egišhurankia seems to have been located near Imgur-Enlil, along the northern wall.

name: Église Saint-Vincent de Marimbault
description: A 12th-century CE church thought to have been built on the site of a Gallo-Roman villa. It is located in the modern French commune of Marimbault (Gironde).

name: Ehilikalamma
description: The temple of the goddess Ašratu, Ehilikalamma (“House of the Luxuriance of the Land”), was located in the Ka-dingirra district (modern Sahn) of Babylon. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, this was one of four temples in that part of the city, which was located in the northern part of the eastern half of Babylon.

name: Ehulhul
description: The temple of the god Sîn at Harran. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ehulhul means "House which Gives Joy."

name: Ehursagtilla
description: The temple of the god Ninurta, Ehursagtilla (“House Which Exterminates the Mountains”), was located in the Šuanna district (modern Ishin Aswad) of Babylon. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, this was one of two temples in that part of the city, which was located in the southern part of the eastern half of Babylon. The building was rebuilt by the Neo-Babylonian king Nabopolassar (r. 625–605 BC).

name: Eigikalamma
description: The temple of the god Lugal-Marda at Marad. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eigikalamma means "House, Eye of the Land."

name: Eileithyaia, Zeus Hypatos, Aphrodite?, T.
description: A peak sanctuary on the Kounados hill of Paros, where sacred activity, including votives, indicates venerations of Zeus Hypatos along with Eileithyaia and Aphrodite.

name: Eileithyias Antron
description: The Cave of Eileithyia outside Heraklion on Crete is an ancient sanctuary sacred to the goddess of childbirth. The shrine was active during Neolithic, Minoan, and Mycenaean phases.

name: Ekadima
description: The temple of the god Belili, Ekadima (“House Which Creates ...”), was located in the Kumar district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of seven temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Ekagula
description: The dais of the Anunnakū gods, Ekagula (“House, Big Gate”), was located in the TE.E (read as either Tê or Kisīri) district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of two daises in that part of Babylon.

name: Ekarzagina
description: The temple of the god Ea within the Esagil(a) complex at Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ekarzagina means "Pure Quay"or "Quay of Lapis Lazuli."

name: Ekinu
description: The temple of the goddess Aya at Sippar. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ekinu means "House, Bed-Chamber."

name: Ekišibgalekura
description: The shrine/seat of the goddess Ninimma in the Egipar temple at Ur. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ekišibgalekura may mean "House, Keeper of the Seal of the Ekur."

name: Ekišnugal
description: The temple of the moon god Nanna/Suen/Sîn at Ur from the third to first millennium B.C. Over the course of its long history, it was rebuilt (and expanded) numerous times. A few of its more famous builders were Narām-Sîn of Agade, Ur-Nammu of Ur, and Nebuchadnezzar II of Babylon.

name: Ekišnugal
description: The temple of the god Sîn, Ekišnugal (“House of the Great Light”), was located in the Kullab district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of four temples in that part of Babylon. The temple is mentioned in Old Babylonian Period texts and was (re)built by the Old Babylonian king Abī-ēšuh (r. ca. 1711–1684 BC) and the Neo-Babylonian ruler Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–562 BC). The temple survived into the Hellenistic Period.

name: Ekitušgarza
description: The temple of the goddess Bēlet-Eanna, Ekitušgarza (“House, Abode of the Regulations”), was located in the Tuba district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of three temples in that part of Babylon. Ekitušgarza was rebuilt by the Babylonian king Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–652 BC).

name: Ekitušgirzal
description: The temple of the goddess Bēlet-Eanna, Ekitušgirzal (“House, Abode of Joy”), was located in the New City district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of two temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Ekituškuga
description: A ziggurat dedicated to the goddess Ištar was built near the center of the citadel of Nineveh, not far from the city’s main temple Emašmaš. This structure was given the Sumerian ceremonial names Ekituškuga ("House, Pure Abode") and Ekibikuga ("House whose Site is Pure").

name: Ekunankuga
description: The ziggurat of the god Šamaš at Sippar. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ekunankuga means "House, Pure Stairway of Heaven."

name: Ekurra
description: The temple of the god Bunene at Sippar. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ekurra means "House of the Mountains."

name: El Bovalar
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F4 El Bovalar

name: el Hofra
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 31 F4 el Hofra

name: el Qassabat el-Gharbiya
description: An ancient place, cited: None

name: el-Arba'in
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 E4 el-Arba'in

name: el-Chatatba
description: A ruined temple built in Cipollino marble located about 10 km south of Sabratha.

name: el-Fra'iyeh
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D5 el-Fra'iyeh

name: el-Garrusc
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 37 B1 el-Garrusc

name: el-Hosn
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 C4 el-Hosn

name: el-Khirbet
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D5 el-Khirbet

name: el-Ma'yen
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D5 el-Ma'yen

name: el-Muftella
description: An ancient place, cited: None

name: el-Nadura
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D3 el-Nadura

name: el-Qasr
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 71 B3 el-Qasr

name: Elephant tomb at Carmona
description: Structure in the the necropolis of Carmona (near Seville) named for a small sculpture of an elephant found inside it. Rather than a tomb, it may have been a sanctuary or temple.

name: Elephanta Caves
description: An island in the Sea of Oman is home to the "City of Caves", where an important assemblage of rock-cut art dating to the fifth through eighth centuries and linked to the cult of Shiva may be found.

name: Eleusinioi Theoi, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 E4 Eleusinioi Theoi, T.

name: Ellès
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 D1 Ellès

name: Elst
description: In the village of Elst, located between Arnhem and Nijmegen, the remains of two quadrangular Romano-Celtic-type temples were discovered beneath the parish church following World War II.

name: Elugalgalgasisa
description: The ziggurat of the god Sîn at Ur. The Sumerian ceremonial name Elugalgalgasisa means "House of the King who Lets Counsel Flourish". The platform temple was originally built during the Early Bronze Age and was then restored in the sixth century B.C. by King Nabonidus.

name: Emah
description: The temple of the goddess Ninmah/Bēlet-ilī, Emah (“Exalated House”), was located in the Ka-dingirra district (modern Kasr) of Babylon. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, this was one of four temples in that part of the city, which was located in the northern part of the eastern half of Babylon. Emah was rebuilt by the Assyrian king Ashurbanipal (r. 668–ca. 631 BC) and the Babylonian king Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–562 BC).

name: Emašdari
description: The temple of the goddess Ištar of Agade at Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial name Emašdari means "House of Animal Offerings." The Neo-Babylonian king Nabonidus rebuilt/renovated it.

name: Emašmaš
description: The most important temple in the citadel of Nineveh was dedicated to the city's tutelary deity, the goddess Ištar. The temple was located in the center of the citadel and went by the Sumerian ceremonial name Emašmaš (meaning uncertain).

name: Emecik
description: An ancient settlement and sanctuary site on the Cnidus (modern Reşadiye/Datça) peninsula.

name: Emekiliburur
description: The temple of the goddess Šarrat-Larsa, Emekiliburur (“House which Gathers All the Me’s”), was located in the Kullab district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of four temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Emesikila
description: The temple of the god Amurru, Emesikila (“House of Pure Me’s”), was located in the Kumar district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of seven temples in that part of Babylon. Emesikila is mentioned in Old Babylonian sources.

name: Emeslam
description: The temple of the god Nergal at Cutha. The Sumerian ceremonial name Emeslam means "House, Warrior of the Netherworld."

name: Emeurur
description: The temple of the goddess Nanāya, Emeurur (“House which Gathers the Me’s”), was located in the TE.E (read as either Tê or Kisīri) district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was the only temple in that part of Babylon.

name: Enamhe
description: The temple of the god Adad, Enamhe (“House of Plenty”), was located in the Kumar district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of seven temples in that part of Babylon. Enamhe was (re)built by the the Old Babylonian kings Sūmû-lā-El (r. ca. 1881–1845 BC) and Hammu-rāpi (r. ca. 1792–1750 BC) and the Neo-Babylonian ruler Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–562 BC).

name: Enamhe
description: The temple of the gods Adad and Apla-Adad at Anat (Anatho). The Sumerian ceremonial name Enamhe means "House of Plenty."

name: Enamtila
description: The temple of the god Bēl-mātāti (Enlil), Enamtila (“House of Life”), was located in the Kumar district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of seven temples in that part of Babylon. Enamtila is mentioned in Old Babylonian and Kassite Period texts.

name: Eniggidrukalamasuma
description: The temple of the god Nabû of the ḫarû at Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eniggidrukalamasuma means "House which Bestows the Scepter of the Land."

name: Enirgalana
description: The cella/shrine of the goddess Ištar in the Eanna temple at Uruk. The Sumerian ceremonial name Enirgalana means "House of the Prince of Heaven."

name: Enlil temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the god Enlil. Although its location is not known, the Enlil temple at Kalhu is thought to have been situated within the Ninurta temple complex, in the northwest corner of the citadel.

name: Enun
description: The shrine/seat of the goddess Ningal in the Egipar temple at Ur. The Sumerian ceremonial name Enun means "Princely House."

name: Enunmah
description: The ḫilṣu-building of the goddess Ningal inside the Egišnugal temple at Ur. The Sumerian ceremonial name Enunmah means "House of the Exalted Prince."

name: Enunmah
description: The temple of the god Nusku, Enunmah (“House of the Exalted Prince”), was located in the Bāb-Lugalerra district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of three temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Epaone
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D3 Epaone

name: Epiais-Rhus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Epiais-Rhus

name: Episcopal complex of Eio-el Tolmo de Minateda (Héllín, Albacete, Spain)
description: The episcopal complex of Eio at El Tolmo de Minateda dates to the terminal sixth century CE and was in use during the following two centuries. Its construction may have been directed by Visigothic officials in Toletum.

name: Epoissum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 F3 Epoissum

name: Erebe
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E2 Erebe

name: Erechtheion
description: The Erechtheion is a temple on the north side of the Athenian Acropolis dedicated to Athena Polias and other deities and heroes; the temple is of the Ionic order with several distinctive features, including its famous caryatid porch.

name: Eremo di San Michele (Coli)
description: The cavern of San Michele was a hermit's dwelling place that is located near a cavern known as the "cave of San Colombano". This hermitage lies in a relatively solitary area of the valley of the Curiasca di San Michele stream, a tributary of the Trebbia river (Italian municipality of Coli, province of Piacenza).

name: Eremo di San Michele Arcangelo
description: A rock-cut Christian sanctuary located on Monte Altino in the Monti Aurunci that was begun ca. A.D. 830.

name: Ermita de S. Valero
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F4 Ermita de S. Valero

name: es-Soumaa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 31 F4 es-Soumaa

name: Esabad
description: The temple of the goddess Gula, Esabad (“House of the Open Ear”), was located in the Tuba district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of three temples in that part of Babylon. Esabad was rebuilt by the Assyrian king Ashurbanipal (r. 668–ca. 631 BC) and Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–652 BC). The temple survived into Arsacid times (247 BC–AD 224).

name: Esag
description: The temple of the god Lugalbanda, Esag (“Foremost House”), was located in the Kullab district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of four temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Esagila
description: The temple of the god Marduk at Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial name Esagil(a) means "House, Whose Top is High."

name: Ešahula
description: The temple of the god Nergal at Me-Turnat. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ešahula means "House of the Happy Heart."

name: Ešahulezenzagmukam
description: Around 690 B.C., the Neo-Assyrian king Sennacherib started construction on a New Year’s Temple for Nineveh’s tutelary deity Ištar. Ešahulezenzagmukam, whose Sumerian ceremonial name means "House of Joy and Gladness for the Festival of the Beginning of the Year" was never completed and work on it appears to have come to a complete stop ca. 688 B.C., when Sennacherib decided to rebuild the Akītu Temple at Ashur instead.

name: Ešasurra
description: The temple of the god Išhara, Ešasurra (“House of the Womb”), was located in the Šuanna district (modern Ishin Aswad) of Babylon. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, this was one of two temples in that part of the city, which was located in the southern part of the eastern half of Babylon.

name: Ešiddukišara
description: The temple of the god Nabû, Ešiddukišara (“House of the Director of the Universe”), was located in the Tuba district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of three temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Esterzili
description: The Domu de Orgia at Esterzili is the largest nuragic "megaron" structure thus far discovered in Sardinia.

name: Estrées-St-Denis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Estrées-St-Denis

name: Etchmiadzin Cathedral
description: A fourth century A.D. church located in Ejmiatsin, Armenia. This is the central cathedral of the Mother See of Holy Etchmiadzin of the Armenian Apostolic Church and was enrolled as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2000.

name: Etemenanki
description: The ziggurat of the god Marduk at Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial name Etemenanki means "House, Foundation Platform of Heaven and Underworld."

name: Etruscan acropolis at Volaterrae
description: The multi-phase acropolis of Etruscan Volaterrae has numerous important phases, beginning in the Archaic period. The monumental site continued to develop and grow during the Hellenistic period. It was identified archaeologically only in the nineteenth century. In the twentieth century it was the subject of major investigations under M. Cristofani and, later, M. Bonamici.

name: Etruscan altar of Grotta Porcina
description: A rock-cut altar dating to the first half of the sixth century B.C.

name: Etruscan sanctuary of San Lorenzo Nuovo
description: An Etruscan sanctuary located on Monte Landro northwest of Lake Bolsena.

name: Eulmaš
description: The temple of the goddess Anunnītu at Sippar-Anunnītu (=Sippar-Amnanum).

name: Euphrasian Basilica
description: An Early Byzantine Christian basilica located in Poreč, Croatia.

name: Eurguba
description: The temple of the god Pisangunuk, Eurguba (“House, Established Foundation”), was located in the Kullab district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of four temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Eurmeiminanki
description: The ziggurat of the god the god Nabû at Borsippa. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eurmeiminanki means "House which Gathers the Seven (Me's) of Heaven and Underworld."

name: Eurunanam
description: The temple of the god Nabû, Eurunanam (“House, the Very City”), was located in the New City district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was the only dais in that part of Babylon.

name: Eurykydeion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 unlocated Eurykydeion

name: Eurysakeion
description: A sanctuary dedicated to Eurysakes, son of Ajax on the Kolonos Agoraios in Athens, which served as the central sanctuary of the tribe Aiantis. It has not been located archaeologically, but the findspots of related inscriptions suggest it was somewhere southwest of the Hephaisteion.

name: Euthymios
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 G2 Euthymios

name: Ezida
description: The temple of the god Marduk as Tutu, later of the god Nabû, at Borsippa. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ezida means "True House."

name: Ezida
description: One of the temples in the citadel of Nineveh that was dedicated to Nabû, the god of scribes; it is located north of the temple of the goddess Ištar (Emašmaš). The Sumerian ceremonial name Ezida means "True House."

name: Ezida (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the god Nabû. Unlike most of the religious buildings constructed at Kalhu, the Ezida ("True House") was built in the southeast corner of the Citadel.

name: Eʾesirkalama
description: The temple of the god Pisangunuk, Eʾesirkalama (“House of the Street of the Land”), was located in the Kumar district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of seven temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Eʾešmah
description: The temple of the god Ea, Eʾešmah (“Exalted House”), was located in the Kumar district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of seven temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Fakhrikah
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 H4 Fakhrikah

name: Fanum Carisi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 48 B2 Fanum Carisi

name: Fanum Pithiviers-le-Vieil
description: The Fanum of Pithiviers-le-Vieil.

name: Fanum Voltumnae
description: A shrine of Voltumna that served as the chief religious center of Etruria. The Etruscan League (duodecim populi Etruriae) was said to meet annually at the shrine. Archaeological excavations of the site of Campo della Fiera have been posited as the definitive location of the Fanum Voltumnae.

name: Faras Cathedral
description: The cathedral in Faras, founded in 620 and rebuilt in the 8th and 10th centuries, respectively, was the seat of the Diocese of Faras. The site had well-preserved wall paintings from the 8th to 9th centuries. The cathedral was flooded by the creation of Lake Nasser in the 1960s.

name: Farfa Abbey
description: A Benedictine abbey located ca. 60 km from Rome.

name: Farley Heath
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G3 Farley Heath

name: Farrashband 1
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Farrashband 1

name: Farrashband 2
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Farrashband 2

name: Farrashband 3
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Farrashband 3

name: Farrashband 4
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Farrashband 4

name: Farrashband 5
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Farrashband 5

name: Farsh el-Qasr
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 E4 Farsh el-Qasr

name: Farsh Habash
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D5 Farsh Habash

name: Faverolles
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 18 C2 Faverolles

name: Feudo Nobile
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 E4 Feudo Nobile

name: Filitosa
description: Filitosa is a megalithic site in southern Corsica that was in use from the Neolithic period until Roman times. A number of menhirs have been discovered at the site.

name: Fines
description: A Gallo-Roman site lying along the Roman road from Lyon to Saintes.

name: Fiume di Mallo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 E4 Fiume di Mallo

name: Five Rathas
description: The Five Rathas is the modern name of a group of five seventh-century Pallava rock-cut temples at Mahābalipuram.

name: Flag Fen
description: Flag Fen is a Bronze Age ritual site near Peterborough, England, comprised of 60,000 timbers arranged in five rows that connect Whittlesey Island with Peterborough. Dendrochronological evidence has dates the site to ca. 1365–967 BC.

name: Fluy
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Fluy

name: Fons Clitumni
description: Originally the site of a shrine dedicated to the river god Clitumnus and later a paleochristian shrine. The temple was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011.

name: Fontana Calda di Butera
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 E4 Fontana Calda di Butera

name: Fontans
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 A4 Fontans

name: Fonte Romito
description: A pre-Roman and Roman settlement of Samnium.

name: Fontecchio
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 F4 Fontecchio

name: Forggensee
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 C2 Forggensee

name: Fortuna
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 27 D3 Fortuna

name: Fortuna Muliebris, T.
description: A sanctuary dedicated to Fortuna Muliebris along the Via Latina. Tradition holds that it was dedicated in 487 B.C.

name: Forum Cambusis?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 B3 Forum Cambusis?

name: Forum Cassii
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 C4 Forum Cassii

name: Fountain of Anna Perenna
description: Sacred to the goddess Anna Perenna and the nymphs, this rectangular fountain surrounded by rupestral inscriptions was excavated on the modern Via Guidubaldo del Monte in Rome's Pinciano neighborhood during the late 20th century. Rich votive deposits complement the picture of the goddess' worship painted by Ovid in the Fasti entry for the Ides of March.

name: Fox-Amphoux
description: At Fox-Amphoux the remains of a Roman villa were discovered, adjacent to those of a sanctuary ad fines that may have been dedicated to the Capitoline triad. The site was in use from the time of Augustus until the third century AD.

name: Foz de Colares
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 A3 Foz de Colares

name: Francavilla di Sicilia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 G3 Francavilla di Sicilia

name: Frascole, S. Martino a Poggio
description: A stips votiva was discovered at the site of the Christian church of San Martino a Poggio - Frascole in 1887. The main ancient activity at the site likely took place during the middle of the third century B.C.

name: Freienbach
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 A2 Freienbach

name: Friars Wash Roman Temple
description: The Friars Wash Roman Temple was noted first in 1965. Aerial photography and subsequent archaeological investigation identified a system of ditches and two rectangular structures that were considered to be Romano-British temples.

name: Frontone
description: An Umbro-Roman temple of Iuppiter on the slopes of Monte Catria.

name: Funerary chapel of Amenirdis I
description: Funerary chapel of the Divine Adoratrice of Amun Amenirdis I, daughter of King Kashta.

name: Funerary chapel of Ankhnesneferibre
description: Presumed remains of the funerary chapel of the Divine Adoratrice of Amun Ankhnesneferibre.

name: Funerary chapel of Shepenupet I
description: Funerary chapel of the Divine Adoratrice of Amun Shepenupet I, daughter of King Osorkon III.

name: Funerary chapel of Shepenupet II, Nitocris I and Mehytenweskhet
description: Funerary chapel of the Divine Adoratrice of Amun Shepenupet II, daughter of King Piankhi. Enlarged by her successor, the Divine Adoratrice of Amun Nitocris I, daughter of King Psamtik I, to accommodate her cult and that of her mother Mehytenweskhet.

name: Funerary temple of Amenhotep II
description: Temple dedicated to the cult of King Amenhotep II located on the west bank of Thebes.

name: Funerary temple of Ay and Horemheb
description: Temple dedicated to the cult of Kings Ay and Horemheb located on the west bank of Thebes.

name: Funerary temple of Seti I
description: Temple dedicated to the cult of King Seti I located on the west bank of Thebes.

name: Funerary temple of Siptah and Twosret
description: Temple dedicated to the cult of King Siptah and the Queen Twosret located on the west bank of Thebes.

name: Funerary temple of Thutmose III
description: Temple dedicated to the cult of King Thutmose III located on the west bank of Thebes.

name: Funerary temple of Thutmose IV
description: Temple dedicated to the cult of King Thutmose IV located on the west bank of Thebes.

name: Furfo
description: A vicus of the Vestini located in the upper Aterno valley in Abruzzo. The ancient settlement was located some 15 km south of modern L'Aquila, in the vicinity of S. Maria di Forfona near Barisciano. Furfo lay on the Via Claudia Nova between Amiternum and Peltuinum.

name: Furzol
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C2 Furzol

name: Gabrias
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 A4 Gabrias

name: Gallarus Oratory
description: An early Christian church in Ireland, originally built between the sixth and the ninth centuries.

name: Gallo-Roman fanum at Lapanouse-de-Cernon
description: The remains of a possible Gallo-Roman fanum were recovered, including an enclosure surrounding a rectangular structure along with terra sigillata from La Graufesenque.

name: Gallo-Roman fanum at Recoules-Prévinquières
description: The fanum of a Gallo-Roman cult that was in use from the first century B.C. to the fourth century A.D.

name: Gallo-Roman remains at La Tour, Sablé-sur-Sarthe
description: The remains of a Gallo-Roman sanctuary.

name: Gallo-Roman Sanctuary at Bû
description: Remains of a rectangular Romano-Celtic temple were excavated north of the French town of Bû (in the department of Eure-et-Loir), beginning in the 1960s. Other buildings of uncertain nature and a theater were also identified in the vicinity. Coins and pottery discovered at the site indicate there was activity there during the Roman imperial period.

name: Gallo-Roman Sanctuary at Hanches
description: A Gallo-Roman sanctuary identified in the 1960s at La Cavée du Moulin near Hanches in the Eure-et-Loir department of France.

name: Gallo-Roman sites at Érome, France
description: A pair of archaeological sites in Gallia Narbonensis. The site of Les Orpaillots shows traces of a sanctuary and a group of tombs while Notre-Dame-de-la-Mure showed traces of a late third century Gallo-Roman villa.

name: Gallo-Roman Structure at Moissac
description: A Gallo-Roman structure including hypocaust and other remains of a bath, located beneath the later Church of Saint Martin in modern Moissac, France. The bath may have belonged to a villa of the fourth-fifth centuries CE.

name: Gallo-Roman temple at Nasium
description: Archaeological remains of the Gallo-Roman temple at ancient Nasium, located at Mazeroie, commune Naix-aux-Forges

name: Gallo-Roman temple at Vannes
description: Remains of a Gallo-Roman at Vannes.

name: Gallo-Roman temple of Forêt d'Halatte (cne. Ognon, Oise)
description: A Gallo-Roman temple built ca. mid-first century AD that remained in use until the fifth century.

name: Gamaches-en-Vexin
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B3 Gamaches-en-Vexin

name: Gandzasar
description: 13th century Armenian cathedral located near the village of Vank in the disputed region of Nagorno-Karabakh/Artsakh.

name: Ganesh Ratha
description: Modern name of a seventh-century Pallava rock-cut temple at Mahābalipuram.

name: Gantiadi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 F1 Gantiadi

name: Garden of Cybele
description: An archaeological area in Vienne that includes remains of municipal and domestic architecture.

name: Garian
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F2 Garian

name: Garizein (mountain)
description: One of two mountains in the immediate vicinity of the West Bank city of Nablus.

name: Garni temple
description: The Garni Temple dates to the first century A.D. and was sacred to the Armenian sun god Mihr.

name: Gasr ar-Rakam
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 D1 Gasr ar-Rakam

name: Gasr Bu Zayd
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 D1 Gasr Bu Zayd

name: Gasr el-Gatres
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 B1 Gasr el-Gatres

name: Gasr el-Gezira
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E2 Gasr el-Gezira

name: Gasr Silu
description: Gasr Silu, Libya, is an ancient site documented by the British School at Rome under John Bryan Ward-Perkins in 1969. Field study was conducted by Sheila Gibson and Margaret Browne. Photographs in the BSR archive document architectural remains at the site that include productive installations and the remains of two Byzantine churches. A Byzantine fort (gasr) was also identified.

name: Gasr Stablous
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 C1 no. 13 (Gasr Stablous)

name: Gasr Zeitun
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 D1 Gasr Zeitun

name: Gastaldaga area and the Episcopal complex
description: A Longobard complex in Cividale del Friuli.

name: Gebel Tafnis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D4 Gebel Tafnis

name: Gebel Teir
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D2 Gebel Teir

name: Gelati Monastery
description: The Monastery of Gelati was founded in 1106 in western Georgia. It was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1984.

name: Geran
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F2 Geran

name: Gerasimos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 H2 Gerasimos

name: Germeshin
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D4 Germeshin

name: Geronisos
description: A small island lying off the west coast of Cyprus where a sanctuary has been identified.

name: Geth Rabbi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 E4 Geth Rabbi

name: Ġgantija
description: Megalithic temple on the island of Gozo; named a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1980

name: Ghulbiyan
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 98 F3 Ghulbiyan

name: Giado
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E3 Giado

name: Gialia Monastery
description: The Gialia Monastery is a ruined medieval Georgian Orthodox monastery located in the village of Gialia, Paphos District, northwestern Cyprus.

name: Gilan Gharbi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 A2 Gilan Gharbi

name: Giza
description: The site of Giza encompasses the Giza Necropolis, a large archaeological site famous for its pyramid complexes and monumental sculpture.

name: Glak, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 D2 Glak, Mon.

name: Glale
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 E3 Glale

name: Glava Panega
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 B5 Glava Panega

name: Göbekli Tepe
description: The site of Göbekli Tepe, located in the Germuş mountains of south-eastern Anatolia, was the location of a Neolithic hilltop sanctuary. This sanctuary, thought to be a series of stone enclosures with T-shaped pillars carrying carving of wild animals, was built in the Pre-Pottery Neolithic and ranges in date from 9,600 to 8,200 B.C.

name: Gola di S. Andrea, Itri
description: Located along the course of the Via Appia between Fondi and Itri are the remains of a likely sanctuary of Apollo that dates to the fourth and third century BCE.

name: Golyamo Belovo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 B6 Golyamo Belovo

name: Görresburg
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G2 Görresburg

name: Görtschach
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 E3 Görtschach

name: Gosbecks
description: The Gosbecks farm site is the location of a Romano-Celtic temple. The shrine measures 100 meters square and a pre-Roman enclosure lies within the later temenos. The pre-Roman site was likely sacred to the war-god Camulos. Camulos was later synchronized with the Roman god Mars, although evidence for the worship of Mercury was also discovered at the site.

name: Gournay-sur-Aronde
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Gournay-sur-Aronde

name: Grattauli
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 C3 Grattauli

name: Great altar of Athena
description: The altar of Athena Polias on the Acropolis of Athens, built in or before the 7th century BC.

name: Great Church of Hippo Regius
description: The Great Church of Hippo Regius is located in the Christian quarter of Hippo. The church is associated with Saint Augustine, who was bishop in Hippo from 395 until he died in 430 CE.

name: Great Temple of Hera
description: The Great Temple of Hera, or Polycrates Temple, is the third temple constructed at the Heraion of Samos between 540 and 530 BCE.

name: Great Temple of Petra
description: The Great Temple of Petra is one of the major archaeological and architectural components of central Petra. It is located to the south of the Colonnaded Street and southeast of the Temenos Gate and covers 7560 square meters.

name: Greek Temple at Taras
description: A Doric temple dating to the sixth century B.C., located in the modern piazza Castello in Taranto, Italy. It was initially attributed to Poseidon, but may have been dedicated to a different deity.

name: Gremoulias ancient sanctuary
description: A sanctuary -- with remains of at least two successive temples (6th C BCE and 4th C BCE), a great altar, and votive deposits -- located on the modern Gremoulias saddle, 3.5km SE of Kalavryta in the northern Peloponnese. The site is not known from ancient literature. Scholars speculate that the deity worshiped may have been Artemis and the site may have been an extra-urban sanctuary of nearby Kynaitha.

name: Grotta Trinità
description: The Grotta Trinità is a cave sanctuary located in the comune of Ruffano, province of Lecce, in southern Italy. The cave was in use from the Neolithic period onwards. In the Iron Age, terracotta vessels with incised graffiti date activity to the sixth century BCE and subsequent.

name: Grotto of the Sibyl
description: A cave discovered by Amedeo Maiuri in 1932 and connected in the historical tradition with the Cumaean Sibyl. The site features in the sixth book of Virgil's Aeneid.

name: Grove of Asklepios at Kos
description: Mentioned in ancient sources, this sacred grove in the ancient sanctuary of Asklepios, located on the Aegean island of Kos, cannot be precisely located today.

name: Güglingen Mithraeum
description: A sanctuary dedicated to Mithras located near modern Heilbronn, Germany, in use during the second-third centuries CE.

name: Guild Temple and Mithraeum of Fructosus
description: An architectural complex excavated in 1938 at the intersection of Via del Pomerio and Via del Tempio Rotondo in Ostia. The guild temple dates to the reign of Alexander Severus (222 - 235 CE). Based on epigraphic evidence, this shrine may belong to the guild of the stuppatores. A mithraic shrine with a patron named Fructosus is to be found in the substructure of the podium.

name: Gula temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the goddess Gula. Although its location is not known, the Gula temple at Kalhu is thought to have been part of the Ninurta temple complex (in the northwest corner of the citadel), east of the Šarrat-niphi temple and north of the Kidmuri temple.

name: Gur-i Dukhtar
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 B4 Gur-i Dukhtar

name: Gur-i Shapur
description: The so-called "Shapur cave" in the Zagros Mountains contains a colossal statue of Shapur I, the second ruler of the Sasanid Empire.

name: Gura Dobrogei
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 F4 Gura Dobrogei

name: Gurzuf
description: According to Hind, a sanctuary dating ca. third century BC to second century AD was identified at the Gurzuf Pass. Silver and bronze statuettes of Graeco-Roman divinities were recovered.

name: Guzana (Tell Halaf)
description: An Aramaean principality and Neo-Assyrian provincial capital founded on the site of an extinct Neolithic settlement. Settled through Hellenistic times, it was abandoned at the end of the first century BCE.

name: Gyroulas
description: A Late Archaic rural sanctuary of Demeter and Apollo at Gyroulas on Naxos.

name: Hagar Qin Megalithic Temple Complex (Malta)
description: Ħaġar Qim is a megalithic temple complex (ca. 3600-3200 BC), included in the UNESCO World Heritage List.

name: Hagia Sophia
description: A major paleochristian, then Byzantine, church in Istanbul; subsequently an Islamic mosque, and today a museum.

name: Hagia Sophia, Iznik
description: Hagia Sophia, Iznik, Turkey, was founded as a Christian basilica in the fourth century CE. Justinian I rebuilt the site in the mid-6th century CE. An earthquake in the eleventh century destroyed that church, and it was then substantially rebuilt following 1065. It is to that rebuilding that much of the present structure pertains. Following the Ottoman conquest, the church became a mosque in 1331. The site served that function until ca. 1935, when it became a museum. Following 2011, it again served as a mosque.

name: Hagia Sophia, Thessaloniki
description: A church to Saint Sophia dating originally from the third century A.D.

name: Hajjiabad
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C3 Hajjiabad

name: Hala Sultan Tekke
description: A major Late Bronze Age settlement occupied the site of Hala Sultan Tekke, which later became an important Sufi Islamic shrine associated with Umm Haram.

name: Halberg Mithraeum
description: A natural cave in the side of the Halberg hill at Saarbrücken was enlarged for the use of the Mithras cult.

name: Hapsasieion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 A2 Hapsasieion

name: Harlow
description: Harlow is the site of a pre-Roman and Roman sanctuary.

name: Harran el-Awamid
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 E3 Harran el-Awamid

name: Hasanaliler Basilica
description: Hasanaliler Church is a 6th-century Byzantine church.

name: Hathor Temple, Timna Valley
description: A shrine sacred to Hathor connected with the Egyptian mining settlement that was extracting copper in the Timna Valley.

name: Haut Bécherel
description: Haut Bécherel is the site of a major Gallo-Roman sanctuary sacred to Mars.

name: Hayling Island Iron-Age/Roman Sanctuary
description: Modern Hayling Island (borough of Havant, Hampshire, England) is the site of a multi-phase sanctuary: the Iron Age shrine dates ca. 50 BCE and the Roman temple dates ca. 55-60CE. The sanctuary seems to have suffered decline and ceased being maintained in the early third century CE, although ritual/votive deposition seems to have continued through the fourth century.

name: Hecate, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 B2 Hecate, T.

name: Hekateion
description: The Hekateion is a monumental temple for the goddess Hekate constructed in Lagina ca. 125 BCE.

name: Heliopolis/Col. Iulia Augusta Felix
description: An ancient settlement in Lebanon's Beqaa Valley, named as a UNESCO World Heritage Site because "with its colossal structures, [it] is one of the finest examples of Imperial Roman architecture at its apogee."

name: Henchir Krebita
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 B1 Henchir Krebita

name: Henchir Oum el-Abbas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 B1 Henchir Oum el-Abbas

name: Henchir Oum Iala
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F3 Henchir Oum Iala

name: Henchir Snem
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 C2 Henchir Snem

name: Henley Wood
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 E3 Henley Wood

name: Hephaisteion
description: The temple of Hephaistos located on the northwest side of the Agora of Athens dates to the middle of the fifth century B.C.

name: Hephaistia
description: An ancient settlement on the northern coast of the Greek island of Lemnos.

name: Hephaistion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 65 D5 Hephaistion

name: Hera temple
description: The so-called 'Tavole Palatine' is the site of the ruins of a sixth century B.C. Doric temple sacred to Hera and associated with the city of Metapontum.

name: Hera, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 B3 Hera, T.

name: Heraion
description: The Heraion of Perachora.

name: Heraion
description: The sanctuary of Hera on Samos.

name: Heraion (Argolis)
description: The Argive Heraion was a major ancient sanctuary sacred to Hera located in the Argolid.

name: Herakleion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 59 C2 Herakleion

name: Herakleion
description: An ancient sanctuary of Hercules (Melqart) that Strabo places at one end of the long island (Cotinussa) that extended south from ancient Gades. Significant landscape and coastal change have occurred since antiquity, disrupting the integrity of these islands. Nonetheless, modern scholars have consistently identified the location of the sanctuary as at or near the 17th-18th century Castillio de Sancti Petri, which today occupies a separate homonymous islet. Archaeological finds possibly associated with the sanctuary have been made in the vicinity (both terrestrial and underwater) over the years, but precise location of foundations or other identifiable architecture have remained elusive.

name: Herakles, T.
description: A sanctuary of Herakles located on the island of Lemnos, active from the Archaic period through to the time of the Roman empire.

name: Hermai
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 D3 Hermai

name: Hermel
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 B5 Hermel

name: Hermes and Aphrodite Sanctuary
description: This large, open-air sanctuary near Syme Viannou in southern Crete was in use from 2000 BCE until at least the sixth century CE. It attracted pilgrims who are assumed to have camped at the site, which is far from any contemporary settlement. Monumental construction began in the early second millennium BCE and multiple phases and changes are evident, including the construction of Christian chapels in late antiquity. Although the identities of the deities worshiped here at the earliest periods are uncertain (as are the saints venerated in the Christian period), the association with Hermes and Aphrodite seems to have begun as early as the 10th century BCE.

name: Heroon of Eretria
description: A triangular enclosure with approximately 19 burials (both cremation and inhumation) dating to the Geometric period (ca. 750 BC - ca. 630 BC).

name: Heroon of Trysa
description: The Heroon of Trysa is a scholarly appellation applied to an ancient tomb (ca. 380 BCE) found at Trysa in ancient Lycia. Julius August Schönborn discovered the site in 1841. The shrine is noted for its cycle of relief sculpture.

name: Hexapyla
description: A sanctuary located north of Syracuse.

name: Hibbariye
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C3 Hibbariye

name: Hibis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D3 Hibis

name: Hiera Oikia at Eleusis
description: The so-called "Hiera Oikia" is a multi-room structure dating to the 7th century BC in the sanctuary of Demeter and Kore at Eleusis.

name: Hierakome
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 G3 Hierakome

name: Hieron (Byzantion)
description: A sanctuary that was sacred to Poseidon and Zeus Ourios located at the Black Sea mouth.

name: Hieron (Chalkedonion)/Zeus Ourios, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 B2 Hieron (Chalkedonion)/Zeus Ourios, T.

name: Hlahlah, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 E3 Hlahlah, Mon.

name: Hochscheid
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 H3 Hochscheid

name: Hockwold
description: A Romano-Celtic temple site dating between the second and fourth centuries AD.

name: Horeb Mons, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D4 Horeb Mons, Mon.

name: Horvat 'Erav
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 B3 Horvat 'Erav

name: Horvat Shema'
description: Ancient synagogue site and burial place of Rabbi Shamai just south of Meron, Israel. The site was occupied in the early 1st century BCE, again from the late 2nd to the late 3rd, from the early 4th century to the mid-7th century, from this point until the mid-9th century, and finally from the mid-12th century until the late-13th century.

name: Hosion kai Dikaion, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 C3 Hosion kai Dikaion, T.

name: Hosios Loukas
description: A tenth-century walled monastery in Boeotia, Greece.

name: Housesteads Mithraeum
description: Temple of Mithras at Roman fort of Housesteads (Vercovicium)

name: Husn Niha
description: Husn Niha is the site of four Roman temples that were constructed between the first and third centuries AD.

name: Husn Sfiri
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 B5 Husn Sfiri

name: Hyperteleaton
description: Sanctuary of Apollo Hyperteleatas, partially excavated in 1885 and 1968. In ancient literature the sanctuary is only mentioned by Pausanias, in whose time it was called the Hyperteleaton and was dedicated to Asclepius. The site is located south of Phoiniki in the southeastern Peloponnese at a place known in the 19th century called Chasanaga.

name: Hypogeum of Ħal-Saflieni (Malta)
description: A prehistoric, subterranean temple; named as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1980.

name: Hyria (Naxos)
description: A sixth century B.C. sanctuary located on the island of Naxos.

name: Hyrkania/Kastellion
description: Hyrkania was an ancient fortress in the Judaean Dessert that was rebuilt as the Byzantine monastery of Kastellion (ca. A.D 492).

name: I Cappuccini
description: The church and convent "I Cappuccini" in Frascati was the site of a Roman villa.

name: Idaion Antron
description: A sacred cave on Crete where it was believed Rhea hid Zeus to protect him from Cronus. Archaeological evidence indicates use as a shelter or dwelling as early as the Late Neolithic period, with religious activities taking over in the Bronze Age and continuing through the Roman period.

name: Idomene Minor
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 D3 Idomene Minor

name: Ilissos Basilica
description: An early Christian basilica on the Ilissos River in Athens that may date to the fifth century.

name: Ilıca
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D3 Ilıca

name: Imst
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 C2 Imst

name: Industria/Bodincomagum?
description: Industria/Bodincomagum? (Monteu da Po) was a Ligurian settlement in the Po valley. Under Roman influence the site became known as Industria, a colonial establishment, in regio IX of the Augustan organization of Italy. The colony is likely founded ca. 124 B.C.

name: Iohannes Urtius, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 C3 Iohannes Urtius, Mon.

name: Ionaion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 unlocated Ionaion

name: Iovis Tifatinus
description: A sanctuary sacred to Jupiter located on the western slopes of Tifata Mons (Monte Tifata).

name: Irru
description: A nuragic shrine with a sacred well.

name: Is Pirois
description: A sacred well sanctuary, Pozzo sacro Is Pirois, is associated with the Nuragic culture. Is Pirois is located in the municipality of Villaputzu, in metropolitan Cagliari. The well is built of schist, and the covering structure is a pseudo-dome or tholos.

name: Ishkhani (monastery)
description: A church and monastery in Tao-Klarjeti, first constructed in the 7th century.

name: Ishtar Temple at Mari
description: Temple dedicated to the goddess Ishtar located in the western section of the city of Mari.

name: Isieiou Oros
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 B5/ inset Isieiou Oros

name: Isola del Tinetto
description: An island in the Ligurian Sea.

name: Isthmia
description: Isthmia was a major sanctuary located on the Isthmus of Corinth, which was sacred to Poseidon and Melikertes Palaimon, and site of the panhellenic Isthmian games.

name: Italic temple of Castel di Ieri
description: The remains of an Italic temple, in the locality known as "Pié di Francia". The sanctuary dates from the fourth to first centuries B.C.

name: Itea
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 C4 Itea

name: Iton?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D2 Iton?

name: Itos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F3 Itos

name: Iuktas
description: A Minoan peak sanctuary on Juktas that served the Minoan palace of Knossos. The site was first excavated in 1909 by Sir Arthur Evans. The site was in use from Early to Late Bronze Age.

name: Iunonis Curritis Lucus
description: The sacred 'Grove of Iuno Curritis' located in Falerii Veteres.

name: Iuppiter Latiaris, T.
description: An important Latial sanctuary of Iuppiter located on Mons Albanus (modern Monte Cavo).

name: Iuppiter Urius, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 B2 Iuppiter Urius, T.

name: Izernore
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 18 C3 Izernore

name: Jandial Temple
description: Jandial is the site of a semi-Classical temple of Ionic Greek design constructed in the first or second centuries BCE. It is the farthest east of any known Hellenic temple.

name: Jebel Moneijah
description: Jebel Moneijah was the site of a Nabatean sanctuary in the Southern Sinai.

name: Jebel Serbal
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D4 Jebel Serbal

name: Jmr-t
description: An ancient settlement, centered on the temple to Amun Nakht, located at modern Ain Birbiyeh/Birbibya, in Egypt's Dakhleh Oasis.

name: Jordan Hill Roman Temple
description: A Romano-Celtic temple dating probably to the fourth century CE. Its ancient name is unknown. The name of the hill on which it sits is spelled "Jordon" in some twentieth-century works, including the Barrington Atlas.

name: Jouima
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 28 C2 Jouima

name: Jvari
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 88 C3 Jvari

name: Kabaköy
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 E4 Kabaköy

name: Kabeireion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 A2 Kabeireion

name: Kabeirion
description: A sanctuary near Thebes where the mystery cult of the god Kabeiros and his son, Pais, was celebrated.

name: Kafr Aqareb
description: An ancient place, located in modern Syria, with remains of Roman praetorium, a temple and grottoes for pagan worship, a Byzantine basilica, and a large communal reservoir. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011 as part of the "Ancient Villages of Northern Syria."

name: Kai Ka'us
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 93 G3 Kai Ka'us

name: Kalabak
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D2 Kalabak

name: Kalabaka
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 B1 Kalabaka

name: Kalecik
description: Kalecik (formerly known as Aralesk or Lesk) is a modern village situated ca. 5 km north of Van Kalesi in southeast Turkey. In the village church, a re-used cylindrical stone (presumably a column base) with a building inscription of the Urartian king Išpuini, son of Sarduri I (ninth century BCE), was found. Furthermore, a re-used stone slab with an inscription of the Urartian king Argišti, son of Minua and grandson of Išpuini (eighth century BCE) has also been found here.

name: Kalhu ziggurat
description: The temple-tower of the god Ninurta, which was started by the ninth-century-BC Assyrian ruler Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) and completed by his son and successor Shalmaneser III (r. 858–824 BC), is still the most prominent feature of Kalhu (modern Nimrud). This multi-staged ziggurat, whose remains are now a pyramid of eroding mud-brick, was constructed in the northwest corner of the citadel, next to Kalhu's principal sanctuary, the Ninurta temple.

name: Kalogeros
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 inset Kalogeros

name: Kalota
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 D4 Kalota

name: Kamul (monastery)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 E3 Kamul, Mon.

name: Kapernabou/Kafr Nabo
description: An ancient place, located in modern Syria, with remains of an ancient church and residential buildings. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011 as part of the "Ancient Villages of Northern Syria."

name: Kapsalaes
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 C3 Kapsalaes

name: Karnak
description: A large temple complex in Egypt, associated with the ancient site of Thebes and Luxor and inscribed together with them on the UNESCO World Heritage list.

name: Karnasion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 B3 Karnasion

name: Karthaia, Athena Temple
description: A Doric-order temple, hexastyle in antis (23.20x11.10 m). The building utilizes stone from both Aegina and Paros.

name: Kasnakovo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 C6 Kasnakovo

name: Kastri Livadiou ancient settlement
description: An archaeological site located 4.5km west southwest of modern Dolichi in Greece's Larisa regional unit (Thessaly). Here, a proto-byzantine walled settlement with three basilicas was built partly with spolia from a temple of Herakles and the ancient city of Doliche. On this basis, the excavators have argued for identification of the archaeology with the attested name; however, the lack so far of in-situ evidence predating the 6th century CE has led other scholars to continue to identify the original Classical-Roman polis of Doliche with the archaeological site at Sarandoporo.

name: Kastro ano Diakopto
description: Abundant spolia, artifacts, and building foundations provide evidence for an ancient settlement in the area of modern Kastro ano Diakopto and the church of Ag. Athanasios on the hill above it to the west. It is probably to be identified with ancient Boura, an attested settlement of Achaia.

name: Kástron Khóstion
description: A complex of structures dating to the Greek and Roman period, with ceramic evidence of prior occupation of the vicinity, on a spur of Palióvouno, overlooking a valley leading to the bay of Saránda on the Korinthian Gulf. The excavators associate this site with the attested classical city of Khorsiai on the basis of epigraphic evidence recovered at the site.

name: Kazerun
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 B4 Kazerun

name: Kekropeion
description: The tomb and shrine of Kekrops, first king of Athens, located on the Acropolis. In the sixth century BC it was marked by a giant Ionic column, which was destroyed in the Persian Sack of 480 BC. The site was partially covered by the southwestern corner of the Erechtheion in the second half of the fifth century BC.

name: Kelisaye Espakho
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 97 B2 Kelisaye Espakho

name: Kenaion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D3 Kenaion

name: Kephalos Island Settlement
description: On the small island of modern Kephalos in the Ambracian Gulf, archaeologists discovered a settlement, a pair of Christian basilicas, and other associated structures and small finds. The site may have begun as a refuge settlement in the late fifth / early sixth century CE and seems to have continued in use, possibly with a late monastic function, into the 10th century.

name: Kepia
description: The sanctuary of Heros Auloneites at Kepia.

name: Kerlescan alignments
description: A complex of 555 megaliths arranged in 13 rows.

name: Khane Gauhar
description: Remains of a Buddhist shrine of uncertain date located near modern Darwishan in the Nawa-i-Barakzayi district of Afghanistan's Helmand province.

name: Khayrabad Bala
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 93 G3 Khayrabad Bala

name: Khentiamentiu, Temple of
description: An unexcavated Old Kingdom temple, attested by epigraphic finds at Abydos in Egypt. Its remains are presumed to underlie later Osiris sanctuary structures and part of the modern town.

name: Kherba de Sfisifa
description: A rural location now in the modern Algerian municipal territory of Djouab (Médéa province) where Gsell and his correspondents (AAA, whence BAtlas) observed Roman ruins including a large building with columns that G. speculated might have been a church.

name: Khirbat al-Mudayna
description: A small Moabite sanctuary within a walled-in enclosure was discovered on the Wadi ath-Thamad in Jordan.

name: Khirbet ed Deir
description: The early Byzantine monastery located at Khirbet ed-Deir in the Judean desert was excavated from 1981-1987 by Yizhar Hirschfeld. The remote site yielded important evidence about monastic life from the fourth to seventh centuries CE.

name: Khirbet el Quneitira
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 G3 Khirbet el Quneitira

name: Khirbet el-Bilaas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 E4 Khirbet el-Bilaas

name: Khirbet el-Knese
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C2 Khirbet el-Knese

name: Khirbet et-Tannur
description: Located some 70 km from Petra, Khirbet et-Tannur is the site of a well-preserved, open-air Nabataean sanctuary.

name: Khirbet Jedora
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 B4 Khirbet Jedora

name: Khirbet Semrin
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 E4 Khirbet Semrin

name: Khondro Teikhos
description: A Classical-period sanctuary at Khondro Teikhos, "said to be somewhere NE of Glossa in Skopelos, Sporades, Magnesia, Thessaly" (according to ToposText).

name: Khontro Bigli
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C3 Khontro Bigli

name: Khorsabad ziggurat
description: The eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) had a temple-tower built on the palace terrace of his new capital, Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad). The ziggurat was located at the northern end of the temple sector, which was accessible from Sargon’s palace, through the Courtyard XV.

name: Khortata
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Khortata

name: Khrysomilia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 C2 Khrysomilia

name: Khrysovitsa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 B3 Khrysovitsa

name: Khurha
description: A partially-excavated complex near modern Qum in Iran. The site was possibly a sanctuary and likely dates to the Seleucid age. There is some evidence that the site continued in use into the Parthian period.

name: Khurramdasht
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 G3 Khurramdasht

name: Khwaja Ali Sehyaka/Sehyak
description: This "Mesopotamian-style temple" site located near modern Dishu in Afghanistan's Helmand province was excavated in the 1970s. Carbon-14 dates and "Greek-style architectural fragments and fine red pottery" led the excavators to date the use of the site from the late Hellenistic and Parthian period to "the beginning of the Sasanian period in the 3rd century" CE. Finds included a fragmentary bilingual (Greek/Aramaic) inscription possibly mentioning a Parthian king Phraates and the Iranian deity Ahuramazda.

name: Kiboreia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 D2 Kiboreia

name: Kidmuri Temple
