description: The Temple of Athena Pronaia, located at Delphi, is a peripteral Doric building (6 x 12 columns). It was built ca. 500 BC and is the largest building in the sanctuary of Athena Pronaia.

name: Temple of Athena Skiras at Phaleron
description: This sanctuary, reported in Phaleron by Pausanias, is thought to have been located near the modern "old town" of Phaleron in Attica. It is not to be confused with the Temple of Athena Skiras on the island of Salamis, reported by Herodotus.

name: Temple of Athena Skiras on Salamis
description: This sanctuary, reported by Herodotus, is thought to have been located on the modern Cape Arapis in the northeast part of the island of Salamis, just north of the modern naval base. It is not to be confused with the Temple of Athena Skiras in Phaleron, reported by Pausanias.

name: Temple of Augustales at Centuripae
description: An early Imperial (first to third centuries) temple at Centuripae that has been associated with the priestly college of the Augustales.

name: Temple of Augustus
description: The Temple of Augustus in Pula is a Roman temple that dates to the time of Augustus. The tetrastyle Corinthian temple sites a high podium.

name: Temple of Augustus and Rome (Ankara)
description: The Temple of Augustus and Roma at Ankara likely dates ca. 20 BCE. It is also known as the "Monumentum Ancyranum", highlighting the structure's role in preserving the most complete copy of the inscription known as the "Res Gestae Divi Augusti", which was promulgated in both ancient Greek and Latin following the death of emperor Augustus in 14 CE.

name: Temple of Augustus et Livia
description: The so-called Temple of Augustus et Livia dates ca. 10 B.C. and was originally dedicated to Roma and Augustus. The temple was located on the ancient forum of the city. By the fifth century the temple had become a Christian church. It was extensively restored in the nineteenth century.

name: Temple of Augustus, Barcelona
description: The Temple of Augustus dates to the second century A.D.

name: Temple of Ba'al at Palmyra
description: Temple of Ba'al (or Bel) in the ancient city of Palmyra.

name: Temple of Baalshamin
description: Temple in Palmyra dedicated to the Canaanite god Baalshamin.

name: Temple of Bacchus
description: Part of the complex of Roman temples at Baalbek, the Temple of Bacchus is one of the best-preserved temples of the Roman world. It is conventionally dated to the second century CE.

name: Temple of Bêl
description: Temple building located on the northwest side of Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of Bel at Dura-Europos
description: A temple building located on the northwest side of Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of Castor and Pollux (Rome)
description: Located at the southeast corner of the Forum Romanum, the aedes Castoris is a temple of ancient Rome vowed by the dictator Postumius in 499 BCE.

name: Temple of Castor and Pollux, Cora
description: The Temple of Castor and Pollux at Cora was originally built between the fourth and the second centuries B.C. The partially extant architrave carries an inscription of the first century B.C.

name: Temple of Claudius
description: A large classical-style Roman temple built shortly after the conquest of Britannia in A.D. 43 by the emperor Claudius. Its remains lie beneath Colchester Castle.

name: Temple of Concord
description: The Temple of Concord at Rome was located at the western margin of the Forum Romanum. Its origins are sometimes linked to a vow made by Marcus Furius Camillus in 367 BCE, but the vow may not have been fulfilled until 218 BCE under Lucius Manlius. The sanctuary was rebuilt in 121 BCE and again in 10 CE.

name: Temple of Concordia, Agrigento
description: The Temple of Concord, located in the so-called "Valle dei Templi" of Agrigento, dates ca. 440-430 B.C.

name: Temple of Cybele
description: The Attalids built a temple at Pessinus to honor Cybele.

name: Temple of Dakka
description: An ancient temple relocated in the 1960s before the completion of the Aswan dam.

name: Temple of Dedwen
description: An ancient Egyptian temple that was dedicated to Dedwen, the Nubian serpent goddess. It was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1979, as part of "Nubian Monuments from Abu Simbel to Philae". It was relocated to its current location.

name: Temple of Demeter
description: A Doric temple ascribed to Demeter with origins in the fourth century B.C.

name: Temple of Demeter and Kore
description: A Punic and Roman temple sacred to Demeter and Kore located at Bagoi, Perdaxius, Sardinia.

name: Temple of Demeter at the Sanctuary of Demeter Malophoros, Selinus
description: The Temple of Demeter was used for the worship of Demeter (Malophoros), goddess of fertility, grain and agriculture. Built in the seventh century BC, the temple was used continuously until its abandonment ca. 250 BC

name: Temple of Derr
description: A rock-cut temple (speos) located in Lower Nubia, the Temple of Derr was built by Pharaoh Ramesses II (19th Dynasty). The temple was relocated to its current position in 1964 along with the Temple of Amada.

name: Temple of Diana
description: The temple in the forum of Augusta Emerita is conventionally known by the misnomer "Temple of Diana", yet was actually dedicated to the Imperial cult.

name: Temple of Diana at Nemi
description: A site sacred to Diana near Lake Nemi, comprised of a Tuscan-style sanctuary and a sacred grove.

name: Temple of Dionysos at Eretria
description: The Temple of Dionysos at Eretria dates ca. 350 B.C. It is located in the northwest sector of the city near the so-called West Gate.

name: Temple of Dionysos at Teos
description: A Temple of Dionysos in the Ionic order begun in the Hellenistic period.

name: Temple of Divus Augustus (Nola)
description: According to Cassius Dio, Tiberius dedicated a temple to the deified Augustus at a location marking the site of the princeps' death. Its precise location is not known.

name: Temple of Divus Iulius
description: The temple on the Sacra Via dedicated to the deified Iulius Caesar, authorized in 42 B.C. and built by Augustus, with its dedication occurring on 18th August, 29 B.C.

name: Temple of Ellesyia
description: A small rock-cut temple dedicated to Amun, Horus, and Satis dated to the 18th Dynasty. Originally located near Qasr Ibrim/Premis, the temple was relocated to the Museo Egizio in Turin, Italy, in the 1960s prior to the area's flooding beneath the surface of Lake Nasser.

name: Temple of Enodia
description: Pherai was the main cult center of the goddess Enodia. Today, the foundations of the temple of Enodia (or Ennodia) at found at the site.

name: Temple of Fortuna (Jublains)
description: Centerpoint of sanctuary at modern Jublains

name: Temple of Fortuna Augusta
description: A Corinthian order temple in Pompeii dedicated to Fortuna Redux and likely dating to the late first century BC (VII, 4, 1).

name: Temple of Hadrian
description: The Temple of Hadrian at Rome, also referred to as the Hadrianeum, was a temple located in the Campus Martius dedicated to the deified Hadrian by Antoninus Pius in 145 CE. Some of the temple's substantial remains were incorporated into a 17th-century building designed by architect Carlo Fontana in Rome's Piazza di Pietra.

name: Temple of Hadrian (Ephesus)
description: A small, prostyle Roman temple located on Curetes Street in ancient Ephesus, the Temple of Hadrian was dedicated to the emperor Hadrian in 138 CE by Publius Quintilius.

name: Temple of Hathor
description: The Temple of Hathor at Deir el-Medina dates to the reign of the Ptolemies. It was later repurposed as a Christian installation known as Deir el-Medina or "Monastery of the City", from which the site's modern toponym is derived.

name: Temple of Hathor Miskar
description: A Punic temple founded in the first century B.C.

name: Temple of Hera at Foce del Sele
description: A sanctuary at the mouth of the Sele river in Italy with an octastyle temple dedicated to the Argive Hera.

name: Temple of Hera I (the "Basilica")
description: An Archaic Doric temple probably dedicated to Hera in the central urban sanctuary of Paestum/Poseidonia.

name: Temple of Hera II (Neptune)
description: A fifth century B.C. Doric temple, the so-called Temple of Hera II is also sometimes referred to as the Temple of Neptune.

name: Temple of Hera Lacinia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 46 F3 Hera, T.

name: Temple of Hera, Mon Repos
description: An archaic temple dating ca. 610 B.C.

name: Temple of Hera, Olympia
description: The Temple of Hera at Olympia, also known as the Heraion, is a peripteral temple located in the northwest corner of the Altis precinct, dedicated to Hera. Built ca. 600-590 B.C., the Heraion is the oldest temple at Olympia and one of the earliest known examples of the Doric order in Greece.

name: Temple of Heracles
description: The Temple of Heracles is one of the oldest temples in the Valley of the Temples in ancient Akragas, Sicily, dating from around the late 6th century BCE.

name: Temple of Herakles, Cora
description: The Temple of Herakles at Cora was built during the 80s B.C. The temple is tetrastyle in its plan and built following the Etrusco-Italic temple tradition.

name: Temple of Hercules
description: Remains of the fourth-century BCE Temple of Hercules are preserved in the edifice of San Marco d'Alunzio.

name: Temple of Hercules at Amman/Philadelpheia
description: The Temple of Hercules is located within the Amman Citadel in Amman, Jordan. The temple was built during the administration of Geminius Marcianus, ca. 162-166.

name: Temple of Hercules at Montorio al Vomano
description: A sanctuary of Hercules located in the territory of Teramo near the Vomano river.

name: Temple of Hercules Victor
description: A monopteros in the Forum Boarium at Rome built in the late second century BC, perhaps by L. Mummius Achaicus.

name: Temple of Hercules, Lanuvium
description: The Temple of Hercules at Lanuvium.

name: Temple of Hermes at Çatıören
description: Temple of Hermes at Çatıören

name: Temple of Hibis
description: A large temple located in the Kharga Oasis that is dated to the Saite-Persian period.

name: Temple of Horus at Edfu
description: Monumental Ptolemaic temple dedicated to Horus, constructed between 237 BCE and 57 BCE at the settlement of Apollinopolis Magna.

name: Temple of Ishtar at Assur
description: From the late Early Dynastic period (ED III, ca. 2500/2400 BC) until 614 BC, there was a temple in the city of Assur dedicated to Ištar, a goddess who was revered in that important northern-Mesopotamian city in a few different forms. Several building phases (designated “Temples H–A,” from earliest to latest) were excavated by W. Andrae between 1905 and 1914. Periodically the location and orientation of the Ištar temple changed, especially during the reigns of the Middle Assyrian kings Tukultī-Ninurta I (1243–1207 BC) and Aššur-rēša-iši I (1132–1115 BC). The earliest and latest textual records for the existence of the temple date respectively to the time of Ititi, a local ruler of Assur during the Old Akkadian Period, and the reign of Sîn-šarra-iškun (626–612 BC), the penultimate king of Assyria.

name: Temple of Isis
description: The Temple of Isis in Pompeii (VIII, 7, 28) is a sanctuary sacred to Isis that dates from the second century BC.

name: Temple of Iuno Moneta
description: A major ancient sanctuary of the Capitoline Hill, the remains of which are likely to be found beneath Santa Maria in Ara Coeli.

name: Temple of Iuno Moneta at Signia
description: The remains of the Temple of Iuno Moneta on the acropolis of Segni support the later (tenth century) church of St. Peter.

name: Temple of Iuno Sospita, Lanuvium
description: A celebrated cult-place sacred to Iuno Sospita that retained its sacred significance even after a cult to the same divinity was dedicated in the Forum Holitorium in Rome in 194 BCE.

name: Temple of Janus, Autun
description: A monumental first-century A.D. Romano-Celtic sanctuary located in Autun, Saône-et-Loire, France, to the northwest of the ancient city of Augustodunum.

name: Temple of Juno (Hera Lacinia)
description: A temple of the Doric order that was built at Akragas ca. 450 BCE.

name: Temple of Juno Caelestis
description: Juno Caelestis, a successor divinity of the Punic Tanit, is celebrated by a sanctuary built at Dougga ca. 222-235 CE, and sponsored by Q. Gabinius Rufus Felix.

name: Temple of Jupiter
description: The Temple of Jupiter (VII,8,1) in Pompeii sits at the north end of the forum. It was built in the middle of the second century BC.

name: Temple of Jupiter
description: A temple dedicated to the Capitoline triad is located on the forum of Saepinum.

name: Temple of Jupiter (Silifke)
description: A second-century CE temple sacred to Jupiter.

name: Temple of Jupiter Anxur
description: A late Republican sanctuary of the city of Terracina that is typically dated ca. 100-70 B.C.

name: Temple of Jupiter Heliopolitanus
description: The Temple of Jupiter Heliopolitanus at Baalbek is considered the largest temple of the Roman world. It served as an oracular shrine.

name: Temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus
description: The chief temple of the city of Rome was located on the Capitoline Hill.

name: Temple of Jupiter, Damascus
description: The Temple of Jupiter in Damascus was begun during the rule of Augustus and completed during the rule of Constantius II.

name: Temple of Jupiter?, Via Appia
description: A temple located at the fourth mile of the Via Appia.

name: Temple of Kalabsha
description: An ancient temple dated ca. 30 BCE was originally located at Bab al-Kalabsha (Gate of Kalabsha), some 50 km south of Aswan. It was relocated to its present site during the construction of the Aswan High Dam with sponsorship from the government of Germany.

name: Temple of Khnum at Esna
description: A temple dedicated to the god Khnum constructed during the Ptolemaic and Roman period in the city of Esna (Latopolis).

name: Temple of Khonsu at Karnak
description: New Kingdom temple to the god Khonsu, constructed by Ramesses III and expanded by Ptolemy III Euergetes.

name: Temple of Kronos and Rhea
description: A sanctuary dedicated to Kronos and Rhea in the area of the Olympieion in Athens, attested from Classical times into the Roman Imperial period. A 2nd-century AD Doric temple is probably associated with it.

name: Temple of Liber Pater at Sabratha
description: A Temple of Liber Pater at Sabratha that dates to the Iulio-Claudian period.

name: Temple of Magna Mater
description: The Temple of Magna Mater, also known as the Temple of Cybele, was dedicated near the Clivus Victoriae on the Palatine Hill on 11 April 191 BCE. The original temple was destroyed by fire in 111 BCE. Following another series of destructive fires, Augustus restored the temple, as he records in his Res Gestae Divi Augusti.

name: Temple of Maharraqa
description: A temple sacred to Isis and Osiris of Roman date, relocated before the completion of the Aswan dam in the 1960s.

name: Temple of Mars Ultor
description: The Temple of Mars Ultor was dedicated in the Forum of Augustus in 2 B.C., fulfilling a vow that Octavian had made to the god in 42 B.C.

name: Temple of Mater Matuta at Satricum
description: A sanctuary dedicated to Mater Matuta that was in use between the seventh and fifth centuries B.C.

name: Temple of Mercurius
description: A Gallo-Roman temple of Mercurius, located atop the modern Puy-de-Dôme, commune Orcines in France.

name: Temple of Min and Isis
description: This temple is located at the modern Egyptian village of El-Qala, which is near Coptos (modern Qift). It was built likely under Augustus, expanded by Caligula and Claudius, and survived into the Coptic period.

name: Temple of Minerva at Theveste
description: The Temple of Minerva at Theveste dates to the third century A.D.

name: Temple of Montu
description: Egyptian temple dedicated to the god Montou in Armant, built and enlarged from the Middle Kingdom to the Roman period.

name: Temple of Montu
description: Egyptian temple dedicated to the god Montou in Medamud, built and enlarged from the Middle Kingdom to the Roman period.

name: Temple of Montu (El-Tod)
description: A temple sacred to Montu located at El-Tod, Egypt. The temple's foundations were laid during the Fifth Dynasty and the sanctuary continued in use under the Ptolemies.

name: Temple of Nabu at Palmyra
description: A temple dedicated to the Babylonian god of wisdom.

name: Temple of Nabû of the ḫarû at Assur
description: Sîn-šarra-iškun (626–612 BC), Assyria’s penultimate king, built a new temple for the god Nabû of the ḫarû at Assur. The temple was constructed in vacant lot that was next to the temple of the Assyrian Ištar. That Neo-Assyrian king modelled the temple’s plan on that the Nabû temple at Kalhu (Nimrud) and named it after the Nabû temple at Babylon. Egidrukalamasumu (“House Which Bestows the Scepter of the Land”) was destroyed in 614 BC, when Assur was destroyed by the Medes.

name: Temple of Nemesis
description: A Doric peripteral temple was built in the sanctuary in the middle of the fifth century BCE. Construction began around 460-450 BCE and continued until 430–420 BCE.

name: Temple of Olympian Zeus, Athens
description: The Temple of Olympian Zeus is also known as the Olympieion. A colossal temple dedicated to Zeus, it was built in three stages, beginning in 515 BCE and ending in 131 CE.

name: Temple of Olympian Zeus, Syracuse
description: The Temple of Olympian Zeus is located approximately 3 km southwest of Syracuse on the Cyane river.

name: Temple of Peltuinum
description: The Temple of Peltuinum is located on the forum square; its final iteration was that of a Corinthian hexastyle prostyle sanctuary of the Roman phase of the city when the shrine may have been dedicated to Apollo. The sanctuary also may have had a pre-Roman phase connected with a sacred spring.

name: Temple of Portunus
description: A tetrastyle temple dedicated to the god Portunus and dating to the late second century or early first century BC.

name: Temple of Poseidon and Amphitrite on Tenos
description: A peripteral temple.

name: Temple of Poseidon at Isthmia
description: The Poseidon sanctuary at Isthmia lies 16 km to the east of Corinth. The initial phase belongs to the seventh century BC, although the sanctuary was destroyed ca. 470 BC and rebuilt in 440 B.C.

name: Temple of Poseidon at Sounion
description: The hexastyle temple of Poseidon at Sounion was built ca. 444-440 B.C. atop the ruins of an Archaic period temple.

name: Temple of Ptah (Karnak)
description: Originally constructed by the New Kingdom pharoah Thutmose III, this component of the temple complex in Karnak was repaired and embellished multiple times through the Roman period.

name: Temple of Quirinus
description: The Temple of Quirinus was located near the Capitolium Vetus. The temple was built ex manubiis by Lucius Papirius Cursor in 293 B.C., fulfilling the vow made by his father in 325 B.C. Tradition holds that the republican-period temple was sited on an even earlier altar dedicated by the Sabines. The topography and location of the temple are both matters of significant debate.

name: Temple of Ramesses II
description: The Temple of Ramesses II at Abydos in Egypt.

name: Temple of Roma and Augustus (Athens)
description: The Temple or Altar of Roma and Augustus on the Acropolis of Athens was erected shortly after 27 BC near the east front of the Periklean Parthenon.

name: Temple of Roma and Augustus, Leptis Magna
description: The Temple of Roma and Augustus at Leptis Magna is a large temple (ca. 46 x 20 meters) situated on the so-called "Old Forum". It was initially erected between 8 BCE and 19 CE using limestone from Ras el-Hammam. The temple has a pseudo-peripteral temple and is oriented to the southeast. Reconstruction and restoration in the second century CE used marble instead of the original limestone.

name: Temple of Romulus (Rome)
description: A temple along the Sacra Via dedicated to Valerius Romulus, son of Maxentius.

name: Temple of Sardus Pater
description: A Punic-Roman temple dedicated to Sardus Pater Babai located near Fluminimaggiore in southern Sardinia. The original phase of the temple dates ca. 500 BC.

name: Temple of Seti I
description: The Temple of Seti I at Abydos is also known as the "Great Temple of Abydos".

name: Temple of Sîn and Šamaš at Assur
description: A double-temple dedicated to the moon-god Sîn and the sun-god Šamaš existed at the city Assur from the Old Assyrian period onwards. The plan of this holy structure changed several times over the course of its long history, most notably in the Neo-Assyrian period, in the reign of Ashurbanipal II (883–859 BC). The Sîn temple was called Eḫulḫuldirdirra and the Šamaš temple went by the name Ebabbarra.

name: Temple of Soknebtunis
description: A temple dedicated to a local form of the Egyptian god Sobek, Soknebtunis, at Tebtunis. The temple was built by Ptolemy I in the late fourth-early third centuries and was greatly expanded by Ptolemy XII in the first century BCE.

name: Temple of Sulis Minerva
description: 1st century CE temple to Minerva at the Aquae Sulis in Bath, England in use roughly until 410 AD.

name: Temple of Talamonaccio
description: An Etruscan temple of the fourth century BCE, associated with the ancient settlement of Telamon. It lies on Mount Talamonaccio, across the bay from the modern village of Talamone, north of Orbetello, Italy.

name: Temple of Tellus (Thugga)
description: The Temple of Tellus (Thugga) dates to the reign of Gallienus ca. 261 CE.

name: Temple of the Fabri Navales
description: The Temple of the Ship Carpenters at Ostia dates to the later second century CE.

name: Temple of The Gadde
description: The temple at Dura-Europos was named by excavators according to its association with the city's Gadde/Fortune, but it has been more recently recognized as a temple principally devoted to Malakbel.

name: Temple of the Oxus
description: The Temple of the Oxus is a Seleucid-era temple situated within the citadel of Takhti-Sangin, a Graeco-Bactrian town between the Vakhsh and Panj rivers.

name: Temple of The Roman Archers
description: Temple situated on a raised podium and accessed by stairs, dedicated to an unknown deity, and located in Block A1 at Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of the Severan family
description: Near a bath complex built under Commodus, a Severan complex that included a plaza, porticoes, and a temple dedicated to the Severan family was added.

name: Temple of the Severan family at Cuicul
description: A temple dedicated to the genius of the Severan family.

name: Temple of the Sibyl
description: A second-century B.C. Roman temple located on the acropolis of ancient Tibur. The so-called Temple of the Sibyl is a pseudo-peripteral building executed in the Ionic order. Some portions of the structure, including the side walls and rea wall, survive to the height of the capitals.

name: Temple of the Storm God at Aleppo
description: Bronze Age temple dedicated to the weather god Hadad in the citadel of Halab (modern Aleppo).

name: Temple of the Winged Lions
description: The Temple of the Winged Lions is a colonnaded temple built in Petra in order to honor the Arabian goddess al-Uzza.

name: Temple of Thelsae
description: A well-preserved Roman period temple located in modern Dmeir, Syria. The sanctuary was later converted into a fortification.

name: Temple of Thutmose III
description: The Temple of Thutmose III at Deir el-Bahari was built ca. 1435-1425 BCE.

name: Temple of Trajan at Italica
description: A sanctuary dedicated to Trajan in the 'urbs nova' of Italica that flourished under the patronage of Hadrian.

name: Temple of Trajan at Pergamon
description: The Temple of Trajan at Pergamon, also referred to as the Traianeum, was dedicated to Trajan and Zeus Philios and completed during the reign of Hadrian.

name: Temple of Veiovis
description: The Temple of Veiovis, also attested as Aedes Vediovis, was a temple of ancient Rome located on the Capitoline Hill in a location known as "inter duos lucos" that is interpreted to mean a saddle between the two summits of the hill. The remains of a temple podium were discovered beneath the northwest corner of the Palazzo Senatore in 1938-1939.

name: Temple of Venus
description: The Temple of Venus (VIII,1) is located adjacent to Pompeii's Porta Marina. The site was cleared in the early years of the colony to make way for the temple. Seriously damaged by the AD 62 earthquake, the sanctuary was still being rebuilt at the time of its ultimate destruction.

name: Temple of Venus Genetrix
description: The Temple of Venus Genetrix located in the Forum Iulium was dedicated ca. 46 BCE.

name: Temple of Vespasian
description: The "Temple of Vespasian" (VII, 9, 2), also referred to as Aedes Genii Augusti, is situated between the Temple of the Lares Publici and the Building of Eumachia on the eastern side of the forum of Pompeii. Its precise attribution is still a matter of debate.

name: Temple of Vespasian and Titus
description: A temple in the Roman Forum dedicated to the deified Vespasian that was begun by Titus and completed under Domitian.

name: Temple of Vesta at Rome
description: The Temple of Vesta, located at the east end of the Forum Romanum.

name: Temple of Vesta at Tibur
description: The Temple of Vesta in Tivoli dates to the early first century B.C. It was once situated on the acropolis of the ancient city. Its ruins now lie within the grounds of the Villa Gregoriana.

name: Temple of Victory
description: A Greek temple of the Doric order (peripteral hexastyle), built in the fifth century BC, possibly to celebrate a victory against the Carthaginians. Its ruins are still visible in the archaeological site of Himera.

name: Temple of Zeus
description: The Temple of Zeus is Aizanoi's main sanctuary. The temple complex was built under Domitian.

name: Temple of Zeus at Cyrene
description: The Doric Temple of Zeus at Cyrene.

name: Temple of Zeus at Lebadea
description: The foundations of a temple sacred to Zeus at Lebadea. Pausanias reports that the temple was incomplete, owing perhaps to its over-ambitious size or to the nagging costs associated with warfare.

name: Temple of Zeus at Nemea
description: A partially reconstructed Doric temple, dating to the 4th century BCE.

name: Temple of Zeus at Olympia
description: The Temple of Zeus at Olympia was built in the second quarter of the fifth century B.C. Libon of Elis served as architect.

name: Temple of Zeus Karaos
description: The foundations of an ancient temple located to the west of ancient Astakos/Dragomestre in Aitoloakarnania.

name: Temple of Zeus Kyrios/Baal-Shamin
description: Temple building located on the southwest side of Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of Zeus Lepsinos at Euromus
description: The Temple of Zeus Lepsinos at Euromus is located southeast of the ancient settlement. The well-preserved Corinthian temple dates to the reign of Hadrian.

name: Temple of Zeus Megistos
description: Temple located in block C4 at Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of Zeus Megistos, Canatha
description: A tetrastyle temple in the Corinthian order sacred to Zeus Megistos was built at Canatha by the first century CE.

name: Temple of Zeus Olympius (Jerash)
description: The monumental sanctuary of Zeus Olympius at Jerash dates to the 160s CE.

name: Temple of Zeus Theos
description: Temple located in block B3 at Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of Zeus Trossou
description: An epigraphically attested temple at or near ancient Atyochorion (modern Akkent in Turkey).

name: Temple or Large Administrative Building at Sikait
description: A significant, large structure at Sikait in Egypt's Eastern Desert that has been variously identified as an administrative building and, most recently on basis of finds from the first systematic archaeological excavation of the structure, as a temple.

name: Temple to Nefertari at Abu Simbel
description: The smaller of the two temples at Abu Simbel, dedicated to the goddess Hathor and the deified queen Nefertari. The temple was relocated between 1964 and 1968 to prevent its submersion under Lake Nasser after the construction of the Aswan High Dam.

name: Temple to Ramesses II at Abu Simbel
description: The larger of the two temples at Abu Simbel, dedicated to the triad of Amun, Ra-Horakhty, and Ptah, as well as the deified Ramesses II. The temple was relocated between 1964 and 1968 to prevent its submersion under Lake Nasser after the construction of the Aswan High Dam.

name: Temple X9
description: Temple of unknown dedication located in Block X9 of Dura-Europos.

name: templum divi Claudii
description: A temple dedicated to the emperor Claudius who was declared divus (deified) after his death in 54 CE. The platform temple occupied a large area of the Caelian Hill in Rome. The construction project was ultimately completed by the Flavians.

name: Templum Sol
description: A sanctuary in the Campus Agrippae that was dedicated by Aurelian on December 25, 274, and funded by means of spoils taken from Palmyra.

name: Templum Veneris et Romae
description: A double temple sacred to Venus Felix and Roma Aeterna built by Hadrian on the slope of the Velian hill to the north of the Sacra Via beginning in 121 CE.

name: Tentyra
description: The Ptolemaic temple to Hathor at Dendera.

name: Terrace and altar of M. Nonius Balbus
description: Marcus Nonius Balbus of Nuceria was an important civic patron of Herculaneum. Balbus was a praetor and proconsul of Crete and Cyrene and served as tribune of the plebs in 32 B.C. This architectural complex near the Suburban Baths included the terrace as well as an honorific statue and altar.

name: Terrace of Zeus
description: The so-called "Terrace of Zeus" or "Terrazza di Zeus" at Elea-Velia.

name: Terravecchia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 G3 Terravecchia

name: Thalfang-Bäsch
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G3 Thalfang-Bäsch

name: Thaur
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 D2 Thaur

name: The "Hochtor Sanctuary"
description: A Celto-Roman sanctuary situated at an ancient high-mountain pass in the eastern Alps near Grossglockner, excavated beginning in the 1990s. Its ancient name is unknown.

name: The Deir
description: Monumental religious building carved out of rock in Petra, Jordan which was used for a variety of complex religious ceremonies and customs after the time of its completion in the mid-first century CE.

name: The Great Altar at Pergamon
description: A monumental altar built during the reign of king Eumenes II.

name: The Sanctuary of the Nymph Ionides
description: Healing spring described by Pausanias on the Kytherios River, in Salmone, Elis

name: The Sanctuary of Vastogirardi
description: A sanctuary of the Pentrian Samnites on the flanks of Monte Capraro, north of the modern Italian village of Vastogirardi.

name: The Temple of Athena Polias at Priene
description: The temple of Athena at Priene, designed by the architect Pytheos, is an especially good example of the Late Classical form of the Ionic order.

name: Thea Larmene, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 H4 Thea Larmene, T.

name: Theai kai Nymphai, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 D5 Theai kai Nymphai, T.

name: Theater-temple complex, Monte San Nicola
description: A theater-temple complex that stands within pre-Roman walls belonging to a period of Samnite occupation on Monte San Nicola in the municipality of Pietravairano.

name: Thekoa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 B4 Thekoa

name: Theodosios
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 G2 Theodosios

name: Theoktistos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 G2 Theoktistos

name: Theotokos Kosmosoteira
description: A twelfth-century monastic foundation and fortified church in the modern town of Feres in Greece's Evros prefecture. The structure served as a mosque during the Ottoman empire and was returned to use as a church in the 20th century.

name: Thermai
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 E4 Thermai

name: Thetideion
description: A sanctuary of Thetis and/or settlement in the ancient territory of Pharsalus. Scholars have suggested various locations for the ancient site, including Ag. Athanassion at Kato Dasolophos (formerly Bekides) and the area of modern Thetidion (formerly Alchami).

name: Thisbe
description: Thisbe was an ancient town of Boeotia located on the southern flank of Mt. Helicon.

name: Thistleton Roman Town
description: A small town of the Roman era, with likely Iron Age antecedent and associated farmsteads and field systems, located near and around modern Thistleton, Market Overton, and Kendrew Barracks (the former RAF Cottesmore) in Rutland district, England. This archaeological landscape has been known and explored since at least the 19th century through chance finds, field walking, geophysical prospecting, targeted excavation, and aerial photography.

name: Tholas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D4 Tholas

name: Tholos at the Sanctuary of Asklepios, Epidauros
description: The Tholos, a circular structure in the Sanctuary of Asklepios at Epidaurus, was constructed between 365 and 360 BCE. Polykleitos the Younger designed it, and it features Doric architecture and a constructed labyrinth beneath its floor.

name: Thomas, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 B4 Thomas, Mon.

name: Tifernum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 unlocated Tifernum

name: Tigani basilica
description: An Early Christian basilica dating to the fifth and sixth centuries CE.

name: Tigi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 D2 Tigi

name: Timpone della Motta
description: The Timpone della Motta is an archaeological site located near Francavilla Marittima. The site was inhabited from the Middle Bronze Age onward. During the Iron Age, an Oenotrian settlement flourished here, one that developed into an important sanctuary. The site passed into Greek control in the later seventh century B.C. and was eventually abandoned following the Bruttian conquest of the region during the fourth century B.C.

name: Tininai
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F3 Tininai

name: Titkois
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 D1 Titkois

name: Tongeren Gallo-Roman temple complex
description: The Gallo-Roman temple complex of Tongeren is located in the northwestern part of the city of Tongeren. The site pre-dates the Roman city wall.

name: Tor Tignosa
description: Tor Tignosa, mapped and listed in the 17th century Catasto Alessandrino as a landed estate, is a ruined stone structure located some 8 km northeast of Lavinium. Multiple votive inscriptions were found in the vicinity, providing evidence for an ancient sanctuary dating to between the fifth and second centuries BCE.

name: Torre del Fum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 I4 Torre del Fum

name: Torricella
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 G4 Torricella

name: Tossal del Moro
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F4 Tossal del Moro

name: Tou Batou
description: A monastery built by order of Emperor Justinian I. St. Catherine's at the foot of Mount Sinai in modern Egypt.

name: Toumpa
description: Iron Age sanctuary site located approximately 4.5 km south of the city of Salamis.

name: Touphion
description: Touphion is located on the east bank of the Nile River about 20 km south of Thebes. The ruins of a multi-period temple initially built by Sesostris I were found there.

name: Tour de Gresset
description: The remains of a Gallo-Roman fanum.

name: Trajan's Kiosk
description: The ancient monument referred to as "Trajan's Kiosk" or the "Pharaoh's Bed" is an incomplete hypaethral temple that dates to the second century CE. The monument is currently located on Agilkia Island although it was originally built on the island of Philae. It was relocated in the 1960s as part of the International Campaign to Save the Monuments of Nubia.

name: Trakhonas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 D2 Trakhonas

name: Trapeza/Koumari temple (Achaia)
description: A temple in the Doric order that begins in the sixth century BCE and continues in use until the Hellenistic period. The temple may be associated with ancient Rhypai, at Trapeza near Koumari, Aigio, Achaia.

name: Tratturo Caniò
description: Tratturo Caniò is an archaeological site located in the territory of the ancient city of Setia in the Pontine Plain of Lazio, Italy. An archaic temple sacred to Iuno is known at the site, and archaeological prospective in 2009 revealed that there are also earlier Bronze and Iron Age remains at the site.

name: Tredici Altari
description: A Latin federal sanctuary at Lavinium, consisting of a series of altars that range in date from the sixth to the second century BC and a sacred site (or heroon) connected with the hero Aeneas.

name: Treis Ammoundas
description: A sanctuary in NW Kea located at Treis Ammoundas dating from the Archaic to Hellenistic periods.

name: Triangular Shrine
description: A triangular shrine located at a crossroads to the southwest of the Athenian Agora, probably dedicated to a hero, built in the late fifth century BC with evidence for earlier religious activity in the seventh century BC.

name: Trigh-Tarhuna
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F2 Trigh-Tarhuna

name: Triopium
description: Sanctuary of Apollo Triopios, known as the Triopion, in the territory of Knidos, was the federal sanctuary of the Dorian hexapolis.

name: Tripolis
description: The Phoenicians established a commercial outpost at Tripolis (Tripoli) in the ninth century BC. An independent city during the Hellenistic period, Tripolis came under Roman control in 64 BC.

name: Trophonius oracle (Boeotia)
description: An oracular cave sanctuary at Lebadeia (modern Greece). Pausanias reports seeing a cult image here that was made by Praxiteles.

name: Troulli tis Korfis
description: A hilltop sanctuary, in use from the Bronze Age until the time of the Roman Empire.

name: Tsenti
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 B2 Tsenti

name: Tsimintiri Archaic Site
description: The small Aegean island of Tsimintiri, located between Despotiko and Antiparos, has produced archaeological evidence for a large sanctuary and/or other public complex of archaic date, likely to be associated with the extensive archaic sanctuary of Mandra, located on the island of Despotiko, to which Tsimintiri was connected in antiquity.

name: Tura d’ Urhay
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 H2 Tura d’ Urhay

name: Türkmen Baba
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D3 Türkmen Baba

name: Tutzis
description: Toutzis (Dendur) is located in Nubia, approximately 80 km south of modern Aswan. In 23 BCE the Roman provincial governor Petronius built a temple at the direction of Augustus. The temple was dedicated to Isis and the deified Nubian brothers Pedesi and Pihor. Imperiled by the Aswan High Dam project, the temple was relocated by UNESCO in 1963. Egypt gifted the temple to the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York City, where it has been exhibited since 1978.

name: Tzitzis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 C2 Tzitzis

name: Udayagiri Caves
description: Complex of 20 cave temples in Madhya Pradesh dedicated to the gods Vishnu and Shiva, constructed from the 3rd-5th centuries CE.

name: Uley
description: At West Hill, Uley, Gloucestershire, excavations revealed the remains of a multi-period ritual site. This site remained in use from the Neolithic period to the Middle Ages. An Iron Age sanctuary was replaced by a Romano-British temple dedicated to Mercury in the early second century A.D.

name: Umm Ubaydah
description: An ancient place, cited: None

name: Underground Shrine (Paestum)
description: A small, underground tomb-like structure located in the agora of Paestum, thought to be either a shrine or a heroon.

name: Unnamed Gallo-Roman temple
description: The site of an unnamed Gallo-Roman temple is situated in Thésée, Arrondissement of Romorantin-Lanthenay, Centre-Val de Loire, France. This temple site is located close to the ruins at Bâtiments des potiers de Pouillé.

name: Unnamed monastery
description: A monastery founded in late antiquity on the island then known as Lerina/Planasia (modern Île Saint-Honorat, France; BAtlas 16 D2 unnamed monastery)

name: Unnamed place
description: An unnamed monument, settlement, or villa of Syrtica

name: Unnamed Place
description: An unnamed place of Tripolitana

name: Unnamed religious complex
description: Referred to today as "Gisacum", this vast Gallo-Roman religious complex (with major spa), is located largely in the French commune of Le Vieil Evreux.

name: Unnamed sanctuary at Garenne
description: In modern Moulin des Gaumins in commune Thaumiers, France (BAtlas 14 H2 Thaumiers)

name: Unnamed temple
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 E2 no. 62 (temple NW Anagnia)

name: Unnamed temple (Tre Fontane)
description: M. Cristofani describes terracotta antefixes discovered at Tre Fontane, in the general location of the abbey of the same name. These antefixes form part of a set of evidence relevant to a discussion of the functioning of regional coroplastic workshops in central Italy in the late archaic period. They are dated from the later sixth to the early fifth centuries BCE.

name: Unnamed temple near Solforata
description: An ancient temple located near Solforata, between Lavinium and the Alban Lake in modern Italy.

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 D1 no. 21 (Ras el-Hilal)

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 D1 no. 28 (Siret Bu Hosc)

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 D1 no. 29 (Asgafa)

name: Untitled
description: Unlabeled feature captured from the Barrington Atlas

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 D2 no. 31 (bridge over R. Acquoria, temple and quarries)

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 C3 no. 52 (temple near Ardea)

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 D3 no. 56 (temple at Algidus M.)

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 E3 no. 61 (cult place near Trerus fl.)

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 16 F1 unnamed monastery (on Gallinaria Ins.)

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 F4 unnamed unlabeled ((1 monastery))

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 41 B3 unnamed monastery (Caprasia)

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 41 B3 unnamed monastery (Urgo)

name: Ursins Gallo-Roman temple
description: Site of a Gallo-Roman temple.

name: Vacri Italic temple
description: An Italic temple was identified in 1975 in the locality known as Porcareccia, Vacri (Chieti), Italy.

name: Vagniacis
description: Vagniacis was a small Roman site of the first to fifth centuries with likely specialized religious functions. A series of five temples are situated within a temenos along the Watling Street at Springhead.

name: Vaiṣṇava Temple at Udayagiri
description: Temple constructed near the Udayagiri cave complex during the Gupta period (ca. 4th-5th century CE).

name: Val-des-Nymphes
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D5 Val-des-Nymphes

name: Valley Temple of Khafre
description: The Valley Temple associated with the pyramid of Khafre. The Valley Temple is linked to the pyramid and its mortuary temple via a causeway.

name: Varaha Cave
description: The modern name of a seventh-century Pallava cave temple in Mahābalipuram.

name: Varces-Allières-et-Risset
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 F3 Varces-Allières-et-Risset

name: Vardzia
description: Cave monastery complex in southern Georgia constructed from 1184-1186.

name: Varnenum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G2 Varnenum

name: Vaugrenier
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 16 D2 Vaugrenier

name: Vega del Mar
description: A paleochristian basilica.

name: Velletri-Stimmate
description: At Velletri the remains of a Late Archaic sanctuary (ca. 530 BCE) were discovered beneath the church of the SS. Stimmate di S. Francesco. The site is often referred to as "Tempio delle Stimmate"; Cardinal Stefano Borgia discovered it in 1784. The architectural terracottas from the sanctuary are part of the so-called "Veio-Roma-Velletri" decorative system.

name: Velousia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 G4 Velousia

name: Verbinum/Vironum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 D3 Verbinum/Vironum

name: Vercoiran
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 E5 Vercoiran

name: Vessagiriya
description: Vessagiriya is the site of an ancient Buddhist forest monastery in Sri Lanka. It is considered part of the ruined site of Anuradhapura. The monastery was initially built in the middle of the third century BCE and was expanded in the late fifth century CE. A Roman bronze coin, as well as punch-marked coins and/or ingots, were excavated at Vessagiriya in the first decade of the 20th century.

name: Victoria Church
description: A paleochristian church located at Thugga.

name: Vietheil
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G3 Vietheil

name: Vigna Parrocchiale archaeological area
description: A complex of civic and sacred buildings in the urban area of ancient Caere, including the so-called "elliptical building".

name: Villa gallo-romaine de Barat de Vin
description: Rediscovered in the 19th century and partially excavated in the 1960s, this Roman-era complex east of modern Sorde-l'Abbaye (Landes, Nouvelle-Aquitaine in France) has been variously identified as a villa and a road station. Discoveries at the site included stone walls, a courtyard, a portico, baths, a mosaic floor, and a bordering Roman-era roadway. A medieval church indicated in documents and early maps seems to have used some of the ancient structures. The site appears to be largely overgrown and inaccessible today.

name: Villa Pamphili
description: The church of San Pancrazio was founded in the sixth century A.D. by Pope Symmachus. It stands beyond the Porta San Pancrazio on the Ianiculum. Below the church is a massive catacomb referred to as S. Pancrazio or Ottavilla.

name: Villa S. Silvestro
description: The remains of a Republican temple podium now support a Christian church at Villa S. Silvestro in Umbria. The temple's podium likely dates, in its first phase, to the third century B.C.

name: Villards-d’Heria
description: A sanctuary of the Sequani at modern Villards-d'Heria in France. It is archaeologically represented by a two-part architectural complex comprising (1) north of the modern town the monumental "Pont des Arches", which supported a temple over a watercourse that supplied a large spa, and (2) at the nearby Lac-d'Antre, which supplied the majority of the water for the spa complex and also hosted additional temples along its shoreline. Plans in Vurpillot 2016 and Nouvel 2019.

name: Visigothic basilica at Segobriga
description: The basilica of Cabeza del Griego at Segobriga dates to the fifth and sixth centuries CE.

name: Vittel
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 F4 Vittel

name: Vojšanci
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 50 B1 Vojšanci

name: Vounari
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 D2 Vounari

name: Wadi Antar
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F3 Wadi Antar

name: Wadi Crema
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E2 Wadi Crema

name: Wadi es Sebua
description: The Wadi es-Sebua, also known as the Valley of the Lions, is the site of two New Kingdom Egyptian temples. This includes a temple built by Pharaoh Ramesses II (19th Dynasty).

name: Wadi Lella
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E3 Wadi Lella

name: Wadi Merteba
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 C2 Wadi Merteba

name: Wadi Migdal
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F3 Wadi Migdal

name: Wadi Nagat
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 78 C3 Wadi Nagat

name: Wadi Senab
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 C1 Wadi Senab

name: Wanborough
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G3 Wanborough

name: War Pil
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B3 War Pil

name: Weycock Hill
description: Weycock Hill is the site of a Roman temple excavated in 1847. According to Lewis, this was an octagonal temple with a portico. It dates to the early fourth century CE with abandonment in the fifth century.

name: Windmill Hill (Athens)
description: A now-disappeared terrace wall and rock cuttings were thought to be the remains of an ancient sanctuary of uncertain identity.

name: Woodeaton
description: A Romano-British temple of two phases dating to the first century.

name: Yalburt Hittite sanctuary
description: A sanctuary dating to the Late Hittite Empire, with a hieroglyphic Luwian inscription commemorating the achievements of Tudhaliya IV (thirteenth century BC).

name: Yammune
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 B5 Yammune

name: Yanuh
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 A5 Yanuh

name: Yazılıkaya (Eskişehir)
description: Yazılıkaya (also known as 'Midas City') is an archaeological site in Eskişehir Province, Turkey, known for its Phrygian rock-cut monuments, facades, altars, and niches dedicated to the Mother Goddess Matar.

name: Yazılıkaya (Hattusa)
description: A sacred rock-cut sanctuary of the Hittites. The sanctuary was in use from the late sixteenth century B.C. Many of the reliefs date to the reigns of Tudhaliya IV and Suppiluliuma II in the late thirteenth century B.C.

name: Yedikilise
description: Destroyed Armenian monastery in the village of Bakraçlı where Urartian inscriptions have been found.

name: Yeha Temple
description: The Yeha Temple is alleged to be among the oldest known extant structures in Ethiopia. It is executed in the Sabaean style and may date to the seventh century BCE.

name: Yeşilalıç rock niche inscription
description: The modern village formerly known as Ašotakert and/or Aschrut-Darga is situated ca. 60 km to the east of Van at an altitude of 2600 m above the stream Çaybağı which flows into the river Kotur in Iran. Here a rock niche with an inscription of the Urartian kings Išpuini (9th century BCE) and Minua (9th / 8th century BCE) was found. It reports the construction of a "susi temple" of the god Ḫaldi and the establishment of an offering ritual. In this case, the term "susi temple" apparently refers to the niche.

name: Zacchaeus?, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 G2 Zacchaeus?, Mon.

name: Zar Trypa Cave Sanctuary
description: A cave sanctuary, dedicated to the Nymphs, located on the east side of Mount Ossa's western "Psila Dendra" ridge north of the modern village of Spilia. Epigraphic and other evidence indicates the sanctuary was active from the Classical through Roman periods.

name: Zaraka Monastery
description: The ruins of a Frankish monastery between Stymphalia and Kionia, Zaraka, Korinthia, established by monks of the Cistercian Order ca. 1225.

name: Zarnuqa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 E3 Zarnuqa

name: Zaviet el-Mahgiub
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 G2 Zaviet el-Mahgiub

name: Zekweh
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C2 Zekweh

name: Zeus Aglaios (temple)
description: A sanctuary of Zeus Aglaios located west of Metapontum.

name: Zeus Ainesios (temple)
description: A temple of Zeus Ainesios located atop mount Ainos on the island of Cephallania.

name: Zeus Akraios, T.
description: A sanctuary of Zeus Akraios on Mount Pelion.

name: Zeus Alsenos, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 E4 Zeus Alsenos, T.

name: Zeus Andreas, Ampeleites, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D3 Zeus Andreas, Ampeleites, T.

name: Zeus Antigonios, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 H3 Zeus Antigonios, T.

name: Zeus Atabyrios (temple)
description: A sanctuary dedicated to Zeus Atabyrios located on the summit of Mt. Atavyros in Rhodes.

name: Zeus Bennios, Soter, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D4 Zeus Bennios, Soter, T.

name: Zeus Bronton 1, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 E2 Zeus Bronton 1, T.

name: Zeus Bronton 2, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D2 Zeus Bronton 2, T.

name: Zeus Bronton 3, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D2 Zeus Bronton 3, T.

name: Zeus Bronton Megas, T.
